En: He turns to his companion. "Can you imagine the look on Maurice's face when he finds out the RCM has been kicking my door down?"
Zh: 他转向同伴。“要是莫里斯知道RCM闯进了我的房间，你猜他会是什么表情？”

En: He'll shit himself! Positively.
Zh: “他会吓瘫在地的！肯定如此！”

En: And now they've shown up *in force* to break up our meeting! He rubs his hands together excitedly.
Zh: “现在他们还*强行*闯进来破坏我们的集会！”他激动地搓起了手。

En: And now they've sent one of their agents to *infiltrate* our meeting. He rubs his hands together excitedly.
Zh: “现在他们还派了一名探员来*混入*我们的集会！”他激动地搓起了手。

En: The lieutenant sighs. "Something tells me these young men are not very experienced with law enforcement."
Zh: 警督叹了口气。“不知怎么，我感觉这两位年轻人并不熟悉执法部门。”

En: Hold on, we're not here to *break up* your meeting. We want to *join* your meeting!\n
Zh: “等等，我们不是来这里*破坏*你们的集会的。我们是想*加入*你们的集会！”

En: Hold on, I'm not here to *infiltrate* your meeting. I want to *join* your meeting!
Zh: “等等，我不是来这里*混入*你们的集会的，我是想*加入*你们的集会！”

En: The RCM wants to join us? A quizzical expression...
Zh: “RCM想加入我们？”疑惑的表情……

En: My partner, of course, is acting in a strictly *personal capacity*, not as an official representative of the RCM.
Zh: “这我的搭档的个人行为，并不代表RCM官方。”

En: Interesting. Does that mean you've done the reading?
Zh: “有趣。你的意思是你已经完成阅读任务了？”

En: One leader and one follower, the most ancient power dynamic.
Zh: 一个领袖，一个追随者，最古典的权力结构。

En: There's a great deal of tension in this young man's shoulders, more than someone his age should bear...
Zh: 年轻人的肩膀紧绷着，远超他这个年龄的人应该承受的压力……

En: Meanwhile, his companion inclines toward him, eager to catch every word that dribbles from his friend's mouth.
Zh: 同时，他的同伴靠向他，急切地聆听任何从他嘴角漏下的话语。

En: Just a moment -- could this be the former owner of a certain jacket you 'acquired' recently?
Zh: 等一等——他会不会是你最近‘取得’的那件夹克的前主人？

En: Don't let them see you *flustered*, ssssire. Play along...
Zh: 别让他们看到你*慌张*的样子，大大大人。顺着他的话说……

En: Before we move on, do you recognise this jacket? (Show him the Saramirizian jacket.)
Zh: “先别急着进入正题，你认得这件夹克吗？”（向他展示萨拉米尔泽休闲夹克。）

En: You weren't exactly easy to find.
Zh: “你们可不好找啊。”

En: What am I late *for* exactly?
Zh: “我到底迟到了*什么*？”

En: (Play along.) "My apologies, comrades. I had to be sure I wasn't being tailed... by our *class enemies*."
Zh: （顺着他的话说。）“很抱歉，同志们。我得确认我甩掉了……*阶级敌人*的尾巴。”

En: [Leave.]
Zh: [离开。]

En: That's the whole point, of course. We can't just have *anybody* waltzing in here. Martinaise is crawling with reactionary infiltrators.
Zh: “这是有意为之。我们不能随便放任*乱七八糟的人*跳着华尔兹进来。马丁内斯到处都是反动的卧底。”

En: There are RCM spies, for one. Just recently, someone even broke into my apartment, if you can believe it...
Zh: “比如说RCM的间谍。就在最近，有人闯入了我的公寓，你能相信吗……”

En: To say nothing of Gottwaldians and Econoclards.
Zh: “更不要说戈特伍德人与经济眼了……”

En: The young man's companion reflexively snorts at the mention of Gottwaldians and Econoclards.
Zh: 听到戈特伍德与经济眼，年轻人的同伴反射性地哼了一声。

En: Are you saying you *haven't* done the reading for tonight?  The two young men look at one another.
Zh: 两人面面相觑。“你是想说你*还没*读完今晚的内容吗？”

En: Uh oh. No one said anything about *reading*. You'll just have to wing this one.
Zh: 哦不。这个*阅读*又是什么。你得即兴发挥了。

En: Mmmm. The young man considers you a moment...
Zh: “嗯……”年轻人审视了你一番。

En: He's taking your measure. Like an ideological boxer stepping into the ring.
Zh: 他在估量你。像一个意识形态的拳击手走上拳击台。

En: This must be the *theory combat* the boiadeiro warned you about! En garde!
Zh: 这一定是那个牛仔警告过你的*理论搏斗*！各就各位，预备！

En: We all have to take precautions, especially during these delicate times. What matters is that you've made it, and that you've done the reading.
Zh: “我们都得小心点，尤其现在时局混乱。重要的是你终于来了，而且你完成了阅读任务……”

En: (Just wing it.) "The reading, yes. I've *definitely* done that."
Zh: （即兴发挥。）“阅读，是的。我*当然*读完了！”

En: What is this *reading*? I thought this was a revolutionary group!
Zh: “这个*阅读*是什么？我以为这是个革命小组！”

En: There must be some misunderstanding. No one mentioned any reading.
Zh: “或许有什么误会。没人说过要读什么东西。”

En: (Whisper.) "Kim, did *you* do the reading?"
Zh: （低语。）“金，*你*读过了吗？”

En: You have? Excellent. Let's hear your critique then.
Zh: “你读完了？太棒了。那来说说你的批判意见吧。”

En: Yes, a revolutionary *reading* group. The most ideologically advanced materialist reading group in Martinaise. But it sounds like that's news to you...
Zh: “是的，一个革命*阅读*小组。全马丁内斯意识形态最超前的唯物主义阅读小组。但你好像没有听说过……”

En: Well, this is getting awkward. I'm not sure what you were expecting to find here then...
Zh: “这就有点尴尬了。不知道你本以为我们是干什么的……”

En: No, detective. The only reading I've been doing is right here... The lieutenant holds up his little blue notebook.
Zh: “不，警探。我最近唯一的阅读都在这里了……”警督举起他的蓝色小笔记本。

En: There's profound consternation in his voice. You suspect it's about something bigger than your not having done the reading.
Zh: 他的声音中包含着一种深沉的忧虑。你怀疑这不仅仅是因为你没有完成阅读任务。

En: Maybe they can explain themselves.
Zh: “也许他们能解释一番……”

En: Maybe he can explain himself.
Zh: “也许他能解释一番……”

En: He seems to be wagging the notebook at you, as though he suspects you may have forgotten why you're here.
Zh: 他向你晃了晃笔记本，好像在怀疑你是不是忘了为什么要来这里。

En: I have not had time to seek out pretentious communist book clubs, nor have I done their 'reading'.
Zh: “我没有时间寻找这些自命不凡的康米主义读书俱乐部，也没有做过他们的阅读任务。”

En: It doesn't sound like they've done the reading, Steban.
Zh: “看来他们没有做阅读任务，史蒂文。”

En: It doesn't sound like he's done the reading, Steban.
Zh: “看来他没有做阅读任务，史蒂文。”

En: Go ahead. The young man gives you a half-smile. His companion sniffs.
Zh: “来吧。”年轻人向你微笑。他的同伴嗤了一声。

En: I liked... the characters and the plot.
Zh: “我喜欢……角色和情节。”

En: I thought the themes were... *interesting*.
Zh: “我觉得主题……很*有趣*。”

En: There were... too many words.
Zh: “字数……太多了。”

En: Characters? What characters are you talking about?
Zh: “角色？你在说哪些角色？”

En: They always say that when they haven't done the reading.
Zh: “所有没读的人都是这么说的。”

En: Too many... *words*?
Yes, but if the writer just cut a few I'm sure it would be fine.
Zh: “没错，但如果作者能砍掉一些，我觉得就没问题了。”

En: What *exactly* are you two doing here?
Zh: “你们两个到底是来干什么的？”

En: In the most general sense, I would say we're cultivating revolutionary consciousness.
Zh: “笼统地说，我们在培养革命觉悟。”

En: But more specifically, we're running a reading group, the most rigorous and theoretically advanced materialist reading group in Martinaise.
Zh: “更确切地说，我们组织了一个阅读小组，全马丁内斯最严谨、意识形态最超前的唯物主义阅读小组。”

En: Comrade Steban is a great discussion leader. One of the best at the university.
Zh: “史蒂文同志是大学里最出色的讨论组织者之一。”

En: It's obvious they take this 'group' of theirs extremely seriously. Whatever you do, *don't* compare it to a common book club.
Zh: 他们显然非常重视这个“小组”。不管说什么，都别把它和普通的读书俱乐部相提并论。

En: *YAWWWWN!* Can you imagine anything duller than a bunch of binoclards yanking each others' knobs?
Zh: *哈欠*还有什么事情能比一群四眼仔互相扯鸡鸡更无聊？

En: Is this where I can square off in *theory combat*?
Zh: “这里是我能参加*理论搏斗*的地方吗？”

En: Sounds like a place for intense intellectual engagement. Exactly my kind of jam.
Zh: “听起来你们要组织紧张刺激的智力对战。我就好这一口。”

En: Sounds just like a regular book club.
Zh: “听起来就是个普通的读书俱乐部嘛。”

En: Sounds like a yank-fest for binos.
Zh: “听起来是四眼仔的扯鸡鸡比赛。”

En: We have been known to get into some *spirited* debates. But it's always in service of our larger intellectual and ideological project.
Zh: “我们确实组织过一些*激情四射*的辩论。但最终总归是为了我们更宏大的智力与意识形态命题。”

En: That's our whole *thing*. The world is so shallow, all noise and repetition. We're interested in genuinely radical critique.
Zh: “我们*就*干这个。这世界如此肤浅，灌满了噪音与复读。我们只对真正激进的批判意见有兴趣。”

En: It's really nothing like that. The young man scoffs. "A book club is a place for talking about the plot points of bourgeois social novels and sipping effervescent cocktails."
Zh: “风马牛不相及。”年轻人嘲笑你。“你在读书俱乐部里只能喝着气泡水，讨论讨论资产阶级世情小说的剧情。”

En: It's nothing like that. A yank-fest is a decadent bourgeois phenomenon, a pastime for over-educated office workers who lack proper revolutionary outlets for their social dissatisfactions and repressed sexuality.
Zh: “差别大了去了。扯鸡鸡比赛是一种堕落的资产阶级现象。教育程度过高的办公室白领对社会不满，又处于性压抑状态，却不能通过革命的方式来纾解，于是只能在扯鸡鸡比赛里寻找一点消遣。”

En: Repressed sexualities are something of an *idée fixe* of his.
Zh: “性压抑是他的执念。”

En: But now we're getting hung up on the pathologies of capitalism. Let's move on.
Zh: “我们对资本主义的病理聊个没完。换个话题吧。”

En: One of capitalism's more banal pathologies. But let's move on.
Zh: “是资本主义较为陈腐的病理之一。我们还是换个话题吧。”

En: That doesn't sound so bad, the lieutenant says. "In any case, it's healthier than certain *other* after-hours pursuits I've seen officers engage in." He gives you a pointed look.
Zh: “那听起来还不坏，”警督说道。“不管怎么说，总比警官们热衷的*某些*业余消遣要健康多了。”他尖锐地瞟你一眼。

En: Extremely different from discussing the aesthetic merits of avant-garde literature while quaffing a fine Mirovan red, eh, Art Cop?
Zh: 看来和一边吨吨吨地痛饮一瓶米洛凡红酒一边讨论先锋文学的美学价值完全不同哈，艺术警探？

En: Precisely. We're not interested in senseless parroting. We like to read *critically*.
Zh: “一点不错。我们对无脑的鹦鹉学舌毫无兴趣。我们喜欢*批判性*阅读。”

En: Within the contours of Mazovian historical materialism, of course.
Zh: “当然，是在马佐夫历史唯物主义的框架之内。”

En: I didn't realise they taught radical Mazovian theory in the universities.
Zh: “现在的大学居然还教激进马佐夫主义理论？”

En: “Kim, can we arrest these kids for truancy?”
Zh: “金，我们能逮捕这些旷课的小鬼吗？”

En: Okay, so what does your reading group actually *read*?
Zh: “好吧，那你们的阅读小组到底*读*什么？”

En: I think I get the idea. Let me ask about something else.
Zh: “我想我明白了。让我问你点别的事。”

En: We study all the foundational texts of Mazovian theory, of course. Just last week we finished the second volume of Puncher and Wattmann's 'Innocence of Capital'...
Zh: “当然，我们学了所有的马佐夫主义理论基础。上周我们刚刚学完了庞奇和瓦特曼的‘资本无罪论’第二卷……”

En: Truly extraordinary.
Zh: “真是不同凡响。”

En: And before that we spent six weeks on 'State and Plasm'...
Zh: “在那之前我们又用了六周学习‘国家与凝胶’……”

En: For some reason the word *plasm* catches in your ear like a piece of old wax.
Zh: 不知怎么，*凝胶*这个词像耳屎一样卡在了你的耳朵里。

En: Uh oh, you can feel your attention span rapidly deteriorating...
Zh: 啊哦，你能感觉到自己的注意力正在迅速地涣散……

En: This is fine. You can handle a list. In fact, you find the tedium strangely soothing.
Zh: 没关系。不过是一个书单而已。实际上，你觉得这个沉闷的书单有种奇异的安慰。

En: Force yourself to keep listening.
Zh: 强迫自己继续听下去。

En: We've also read Wertmüller's 'The Mega-Structure of History,' and before that, 'Reál and Reality'...
Zh: “我们还读了韦特缪勒的‘历史的巨型结构’，之前还有‘雷亚尔与现实’……”

En: Communist theorists love puns, in case that wasn't obvious.
Zh: 康米主义理论家热爱谐音梗，这简直不能更明显了。

En: Abelard's 'Un Pays Infernal'...
Zh: “阿伯拉的‘地狱般的国家’……”

En: The original Fizdale translation, not that watered-down revisionist garbage.
Zh: “是原本的菲兹戴尔译本，不是那坨阉割过的修正主义垃圾。”

En: These two deserve the Order of Honour for Bullshitting. There's no way they've actually *read* all this stuff.
Zh: 他们两个应该被授予胡说八道学的荣誉学位。他们绝对不可能*读*过这么多东西。

En: Obviously. He snorts.
Zh: “显而易见。”他从鼻子里出气。

En: But, of course, our *special emphasis* is on the theories of Ignus Nilsen and his followers, especially the infra-materialists.
Zh: “不过当然，我们的*重点*还是伊格纳斯·尼尔森和他的追随者，尤其是亚唯物主义者们的理论。”

En: One of Kras Mazov's closest associates, Ignus Nilsen was a leading political and military figure in the Antecentennial Revolution.
Zh: 伊格纳斯·尼尔森是卡拉斯·马佐夫最亲密的战友之一，世纪大革命时期的政治与军事领袖。

En: Why do you always leave out the good parts? Tell him how he got his *true name*, right now!
Zh: 为什么你总是省略最棒的部分？告诉他尼尔森的*真名*从何而来，立刻马上！

En: Oh, right. During the disastrous retreat from Graad to Samara, Nilsen personally ordered up to 12,000 prisoners of war impaled on sharpened spruce trees in the Samaran backcountry. Thereafter his opponents began referring to him as the 'Apocalyptic Shrike'.
Zh: 哦，没错。在那场从格拉德到萨马拉的灾难性的大撤退途中，伊格纳斯·尼尔森在萨马拉的荒郊野外下令，将12000名战犯钉在削尖的云杉树上。从那之后他的政敌就开始称呼他为“末日的伯劳鸟”。

En: Today, Nilsen's reputation has waned in most parts of the developed world, with the exception of the People's Republic of Samara (SRV), where he is recognized as a founding father. His political philosophy is a variant of Mazovianism called Nilsenist-Mazovianism.
Zh: 如今，尼尔森在绝大部分发达国家可谓臭名昭著，唯一的例外是萨马拉共和国。在萨马拉，尼尔森被认为是一名奠基人。他的政治哲学是马佐夫主义的发展，被称为尼尔森-马佐夫主义。

En: Comrade Nilsen, father of the SRV. (Raise your fist.)
Zh: “尼尔森同志，SRV之父。”（举起拳头。）

En: Ignus Nilsen? He's not so popular these days, is he?
Zh: “伊格纳斯·尼尔森？他现在可不怎么受欢迎了，是吧？”

En: Isn't he the guy who had all those prisoners impaled on spruce trees?
Zh: “他就是那个把战犯钉在云杉树梢的家伙？”

En: The young man raises his fist in reply and gives you a curt nod.
Zh: 年轻人也举起拳头，向你点了点头。

En: Only because certain moralists and their ideological lap dogs pretend to object to his more *maximalist* tactics... as though they've never committed any so-called 'war crimes'.
Zh: “只是因为某些道德家和他们的意识形态走狗假装反对他的*最高纲领派*战略……好像他们自己从来没有犯下过所谓的‘战争罪行’一样。”

En: This gendarme talks just like a moralist. They *love* getting on their high horses about so-called 'war crimes'.
Zh: “这位警官讲起话来好像一位道德家。他们*热爱*站在道德高地指责所谓的‘战争罪行’。”

En: The young man grimaces. It seems he's less than 100% comfortable with war crimes.
Zh: 年轻人皱了皱脸。看起来他对战争罪行这个概念不怎么舒服。

En: He *also* happens to be the greatest communist theorist after Mazov himself. It was Nilsen who first postulated the existence of ideological plasm, which forms the basis of infra-materialist theory.
Zh: “他也*恰好*是马佐夫之后最伟大的康米主义理论家。也是尼尔森最先提出了意识形态凝胶的存在，而这构成了亚唯物主义的理论基础。”

En: Those words again! You've got to find out what this infra-materialist stuff is all about.
Zh: 又在说这个了！你得搞明白这个亚唯物主义是什么东西……

En: 'Plasm', huh? Sounds like a hell of a drug. You might wanna ask about it.
Zh: ‘凝胶’哈？听起来像是什么带劲的东西。你得问问他们。

En: Whatever it is, one thing's perfectly clear: These young students have a much deeper understanding of communism than you do...
Zh: 不管它是什么，有一件事情是很清楚的：这些年轻学生对康米主义的理解，可比你深得多了……

En: You could learn a thing or two from them, if you can convince them you're *one* of them.
Zh: 如果你让他们相信你也是其中*一员*，或许就能从他们身上学到点什么。

En: Let your mind wander.
Zh: 发散思维。

En: Your attention detaches. The voices of the young men fade away. It's a cool evening, you notice.
Zh: 你的注意力抽离了。年轻人的声音逐渐漂远。你注意到今夜天气微凉。

En: Listen for night sounds.
Zh: 聆听夜晚的声音。

En: Look around the room.
Zh: 环顾房间。

En: Out beyond the dull chatter, you catch the sound of a distant seabird, and of course the sound of the large stretches of canvas that cover the missing sections of wall...
Zh: 在沉闷的唠叨声之外，你听到远方传来海鸟的叫声，当然还有那片覆盖着墙壁缺失部分的帆布，被晚风拉扯着……

En: Your eye drifts over the detritus and mismatched furniture. It naturally gravitates toward the pieces of canvas that cover a missing stretch of wall...
Zh: 你的目光越过废墟与不成套的家具。它自然地倾向那片覆盖着墙壁缺失部分的帆布……

En: A patch of ocean turned on its side...
Zh: 海洋的边角翻涌着……

En: A woman's skirt swelling in the breeze...
Zh: 一个女人的裙子在风中鼓起……

En: A wild beast tangled in a sheet...
Zh: 一头野兽被困在床单之中……

En: That must be it. You imagine yourself in a small dinghy buoyed along gentle swells...
Zh: 就是这个。你想象自己身处一艘小艇，在温柔的海浪中起伏。

En: The thought is almost too much to bear. What you wouldn't give to glimpse the bare legs behind it...
Zh: 你几乎无法承受这样的想象。你绝不会瞧那光裸的双腿一眼……

En: Yes, an image to get the blood pumping. An unpredictable power, fighting to free itself...
Zh: 没错，一副令人血脉偾张的画面。出人意料的力量，试图挣脱束缚……

En: Gradually, the pleasant sensation recedes. You turn your attention to the two young men, who appear just about finished.
Zh: 渐渐地，愉悦消退了。你把注意力放回到两个年轻人身上。他们似乎刚刚说完。

En: Slowly, too slowly, the pain fades from your side. You turn your attention to the two young men, who appear just about finished.
Zh: 慢慢地，或许是太慢了，你身侧的疼痛消退了。你把注意力放回到两个年轻人身上。他们似乎刚刚说完话。

En: You let the invigorating sensation course through you, then turn your attention to the two young men, who appear just about finished.
Zh: 令人振奋的感受从你周身流淌而过。你把注意力放回到两个年轻人身上。他们似乎刚刚说完话。

En: And before that, there was Abelard's 'Un Pays Infernal'...
Zh: “在那之前，还有阿伯拉的‘地狱般的国家’……”

En: They’re not primary school delinquents, they’re university students. Attendance isn't compulsory. Besides, we’re not the skip squad.
Zh: “他们是大学生，不是小学里的捣蛋鬼，出勤率没那么重要。再说了，我们也不是下级警队。”

En: The Counter-Truancy Task Force (or 'skip squad') is the division of the RCM that drives around in wagons looking for delinquent minors. It's generally considered a punitive assignment for under-performing officers.
Zh: 反旷课特战队（或“下级警队”）是RCM里的一个部门，每天开着货車到处寻找旷课的小孩。一般认为表现不佳的警官才会被分配去那里作为惩罚。

En: Ha! As though you can call that pablum *teaching*.
Zh: “哈！好像你能把那种过家家的东西称作*教育*。”

En: One thing you learn quickly at university is that you're not going to find a real education in any lecture hall or discussion seminar.
Zh: “你在大学里最快学到的一件事就是，你在讲堂或是讨论课上永远学不到真东西。”

En: We're post-attendance, basically.
Zh: “所有我们已经基本不出勤了。”

En: Exactly. The only worthwhile part of the so-called École normale de Revachol is the library. That's where we've made our greatest critical strides.
Zh: “正是如此。瑞瓦肖高等师范学院唯一值得称道的地方是它的图书馆。我们的批判水平在那里获得了最大的进步。”

En: Did this reading group have anything to do with the lynching?
Zh: “这个阅读小组和私刑有关系吗？”

En: Lynching? No. We're not an *operational* cell.
Zh: “私刑？不。我们不是*行动队*。”

En: Are you sure? My working theory is that *communism* killed the hanged man.
Zh: “你确定？我目前的理论是*康米主义*杀死了吊人。”

En: Okay, but what's your group's stance on the lynching?
Zh: “好吧，但你们的小组对私刑怎么看？”

En: Is your reading group affiliated with the Union somehow?
Zh: “你们的阅读小组和工会有任何联系吗？”

En: So what's your stance on crime in general?
Zh: “那么总体来说你们对犯罪的看法是怎样的？”

En: *Are* there any operational communists in Martinaise? (Proceed.)
Zh: “马丁内斯*有*任何还在行动的康米主义者吗？”（继续。）

En: To be clear: Communism is not an official suspect in this investigation. Because it's not a *person*, you see.
Zh: “澄清一下：康米主义并未正式被列为这场调查的嫌犯，因为它不是一个*人*，你瞧。”

En: You're saying you think the mercenary was killed *by* communism itself?
Zh: “你在说那个雇佣兵是*被*康米主义本身杀死的？”

En: Seems unlikely, from a strictly infra-materialist perspective...
Zh: “从一个严格依照亚唯物主义的角度来看，可能性不大……”

En: What are these levels they're talking about? This doesn't sound like any communism you've encountered before...
Zh: 他们在说的这个等级是什么？听起来不像之前你遇到过的任何康米主义理论……

En: In any case, it's not like the SRV has established a party line on the subject of lynchings in Martinaise... The two young men look at one another.\n
Zh: “不管怎么说，SRV又没有建一条关于马丁内斯私刑事件的专线……”两个年轻人看看彼此。

En: Our stance? What, does he want to know if the SRV has established a party line on lynchings in Martinaise? The two young men look at one another.
Zh: “我们的看法？怎么，他想知道SRV有没有建一条关于马丁内斯私刑事件的专线？”两个年轻人看看彼此。

En: The SRV refers to the People's Republic of Samara. Established as a socialist utopia by survivors of the Revolution, it has since degenerated into a bureaucratic workers' state under the decades-long rule of President Sapormat 'Sport' Knezhinisky.
Zh: SRV指萨马拉共和国。当年那场革命的幸存者们建立了这个工人主义的乌托邦，但它随后堕落成了一个官僚工人国家，经历了运动达人‘体育’涅仁斯基总统数十年的统治。

En: Though historically speaking, the SRV *has supported* direct action against right-wing paramilitary squads, especially when they're doing the Indotribes' dirty work.
Zh: “不过在历史上，SRV*支持过*对右翼的准军事小队的直接行动，尤其是当他们在给寡头干脏活的时候。”

En: Good point. So as a provisional matter, I can say we support it.
Zh: “说得不错。所以目前来说，我可以说我们支持私刑。”

En: It's not that the boundary between sarcasm and sincerity is porous with these two, it's that they actually seem to *overlap*, as though one is meant to conceal the other.
Zh: 这两人的讽刺和真诚之间似乎并没有一道漏洞百出的界线，而是它们看起来真的*重合*在一起，好像一者是为了掩盖另一者那样。

En: Are they being sarcastic? You feel like you're caught in some elaborate joke labyrinth, but it's impossible to see your way through.
Zh: 他们是在讽刺吗？你感觉自己被困在了某种精巧的玩笑迷宫里面，看不到出路。

En: It's always that way. Beneath the crust of irony there's a molten sincerity that threatens to erupt forth... You may witness it yet.
Zh: 总是这样的。在讽刺的硬壳之下，流动着似乎随时会迸射出来，熔化的真诚……或许你有机会看到。

En: One thing *is* perfectly clear: These young students have a much deeper understanding of communism than you do...
Zh: 有一件事情是很清楚的：这些年轻学生对康米主义的理解，可比你深得多了……

En: You could learn a thing or two from them, if you can convince them you're one of them.
Zh: 如果你让他们相信你也是其中一员，或许就能从他们身上学到点什么。

En: No, we're an independent organisation, he says proudly. "We acknowledge and respect the Union's efforts, but our interests are more theoretical than Mr. Claire's."
Zh: “不，我们是一个独立组织，”他骄傲地说道。“我们知晓并且敬重工会的努力，但相较于克莱尔先生，我们的关注重点要更偏向理论方向。”

En: He speaks the truth.
Zh: 他说的是实话。

En: That's easy: Crime is simply the inevitable expression of the injustice and incoherence embedded within capitalism itself.
Zh: “很简单：资本主义社会充斥着如此之多的不公与分裂，犯罪不过是其不可避免的外在表现。”

En: Delivered with the smug assurance of a schoolboy reciting a maxim he's committed to memory.
Zh: 他说这话的表情就像一个小学生，正在志得意满地背诵一条他努力记住的格言。

En: It's a symptom, in other words. Not a cause. He waves his hand as though this is all there is to say on the subject of crime.
Zh: “换句话说，它是一种症状。不是根本原因。”他挥了挥手，好像这就能终结一切关于犯罪的争论了。

En: His companion can barely suppress a yawn.
Zh: 他同伴差点没忍住打了个哈欠。

En: No, unfortunately. The communards were hunted down and killed nearly to a man. All that's left of them are bones and old rifles.
Zh: “不幸的是，没有了。公社成员们被围追堵截，残杀殆尽。他们留下的遗产只有累累白骨与老朽的步枪。”

En: Right. They all got shot in the head, just like the anarchists.
Zh: “没错。他们都被枪杀了，和那些无政府主义者一样。”

En: Like this one. (Show him the Belle-Magrave rifle.)
Zh: “像这把一样。”（向他展示贝莱-马格雷步枪。）

En: This little guy is still going. (Show him the figurine.)
Zh: “这个小东西仍在前进。”（向他展示）

En: Well, that's too bad.
Zh: “这真是太糟糕了。”

En: The Insulindian Deluge wiped out an entire generation of communists. Afterward, they were all bulldozed into mass graves. The young man looks slightly queasy at the thought.
Zh: “伊苏林迪大洪水摧毁了一代康米主义者。在那之后，他们都被扔进了乱葬岗……”年轻人似乎因为这段回忆而作呕。

En: Not exactly the second incarnation of Kras Mazov, is he? The young man considers the figurine with a slight grimace.
Zh: “看起来可不像是卡拉斯马佐夫的转世，对吧？”年轻人作出一副苦相，仔细看了看小雕像。

En: His companion's eyes widen as you hold up the ancient weapon.
Zh: 你举起这把古老的武器，年轻人的同伴瞪大了双眼。

En: It's a world-historical nightmare, the young man says gravely. "One from which we've yet to awaken."
Zh: “这是世界历史的梦魇。”年轻人沉痛地说。“一个我们尚未从中醒来的噩梦。”

En: He's practically itching to stick that thing in someone's face and pull the trigger.
Zh: 他简直忍不住要把这东西塞进某个人的嘴里，然后扣动扳机了。

En: Yes, like that one. I wonder how many of these are still lying around in cellars or sealed up behind the masonry. He knits his brows together, and then looks up.
Zh: “是的，就像这把。不知道如今还有多少这样的步枪，躺在地窖里积灰或是被封在砖墙内部……”他紧皱眉头，然后抬起头看你。

En: Can I hold it?
Zh: “我能握一握它吗？”

En: Here. (Give him the rifle.)
Zh: “拿着。”（把步枪给他。）

En: I'd prefer you didn't. (Put it away.)
Zh: “还是不给你比较好。”（把步枪放在一边。）

En: It's heavier than I was expecting. The rifle rests in his open palms. Its position reminds you of something...
Zh: “比我想象中要重。”步枪躺在他的掌中。这个位置让你想到了什么……

En: Of course. What would I even do with it? He gives you a half-smile.
Zh: “好吧。我拿着它又能做什么呢？”他挤出一个微笑。

En: The attitude of a priest bearing one of the holy relics of his faith. Though it is long out-of-service, its power is evident to him.
Zh: 他的神色如同一个捧着圣物的信徒一般。即使年久失修，步枪所蕴含的力量对他来说依然清晰可见。

En: He doesn't mean to show it, but the disappointment is plainly written across his face.
Zh: 他不想表露出来，但他的脸上写满了失望。

En: When he's finished, he returns the rifle to you with a nod of appreciation.
Zh: 欣赏完了之后，他把步枪还给你，点点头表示感谢。

En: ... though what, exactly, you can't say. He turns the rifle over again, admiring the glint of light off the oily barrel.
Zh: ……但是你看不出具体是什么。他又把步枪翻过去看，欣赏油润的枪杆上的反光。

En: What's it for?
Zh: “它是干什么用的？”

En: I'm holding onto it for someone.
Zh: “我为了某个人而执着于它。”

En: It kills fascists.
Zh: “它能杀死法西斯分子。”

En: What do you mean? It's just a nice figurine.
Zh: “你想问什么？它不过是一个漂亮的小雕像。”

En: Ah, I see. That's certainly kind of you. He gives you a curious half-smile.
Zh: “啊，我明白了。你真好。”他好奇地冲你一笑。

En: Right. Sure he does. He gives you a sly half-smile.
Zh: “不错。它确实可以。”他狡黠地冲你一笑。

En: Right, of course it is. He gives you a sad half-smile.
Zh: “不错。它确实漂亮。”他露出一个悲伤的微笑。

En: His friend, though, seems oddly unmoved.
Zh: 奇怪的是，他的朋友似乎不为所动。

En: On the contrary, he appears to be *savouring* the thought of so many people shot in the head, regardless of their beliefs.
Zh: 恰恰相反，他好像*沉醉于*如此多的人被枪杀的事情，不管他们的信仰为何。

En: Mark it, there's something sinister in that one.
Zh: 记住这点。这个人有种邪恶的感觉。

En: When will *you* wake up? And what will you see when you do?
Zh: *你*什么时候才能醒来？那时你将会看到什么？

En: Where is the rest of the reading group?
Zh: “阅读小组的其他人去哪里了？”

En: What do you mean? This *is* the reading group...
Zh: “你在说什么？我们*就是*阅读小组……”

En: Shouldn't a group have, like, more people in it?
Zh: “一个小组难道不应该，呃，有更多成员吗？”

En: So there's just two of you?
Zh: “所以只有你们两个？”

En: Two's all you need. Me and Kim are the same way. (Turn to Kim.)
Zh: “两个就够了。我和金也是一样的。”（转向金。）

En: Kim and *I*... the lieutenant mutters under his breath. He scribbles something in his notebook but adds nothing else.
Zh: “金和*我*……”警督咕哝了一句。他在笔记本上写下几行字，然后就停笔了。

En: We're in something of a rebuilding phase.
Zh: “我们处在一个重建阶段。”

En: Some of our former comrades didn't have the *ideological fortitude* our work demands.
Zh: “我们以前的一些同志缺乏工作所需的*意识形态毅力*。”

En: Okay, but what happened to them?
Zh: “好吧，那他们身上发生了什么？”

En: And the Gottwaldians, what's so bad about them?
Zh: “还有戈特伍德学派，他们怎么不好了？”

En: What's so bad about the Gottwaldians?
Zh: “戈特伍德学派怎么不好了？”

En: Okay, but what about the Econoclards?
Zh: “好吧，但经济眼又是怎么回事？”

En: Who are the Econoclards?
Zh: “经济眼是什么人？”

En: And what about the liberals? Are they liberals, too?
Zh: “自由主义者又怎么了？他们也是自由主义者吗？”

En: It sounds like Maurice objected to some aspects of infra-materialism?
Zh: “听起来好像莫里斯反对亚唯物主义的某些观点？”

En: Did you say something about *turnips*?
Zh: “你刚刚说了什么*萝卜*？”

En: I've heard enough. Let's talk about something else.
Zh: “我听得够多了。我们聊点别的吧。”

En: Intellectual attrition is maybe the best way to describe it. Felix said he couldn't keep up with the reading on top of his classwork. And Zuzanna wanted to read texts *other than* Mazovian theory. Like novels, if you can believe it...
Zh: “最恰当的说法是学术摩擦。菲力克斯说他没法跟上课业，祖扎娜想读马佐夫主义理论以外的内容。比如长篇小说，你能相信吗……”

En: Imagine, the audacity of wanting to read a novel in a reading group!
Zh: 竟有如此大胆之人，敢在阅读小组里提出要读长篇小说！

En: We've tried recruiting new members, but unfortunately the *current intellectual climate* is pretty hostile to infra-materialist thought. These days, if you're on the left, the ascendent schools are the Gottwaldians and the Econoclards.
Zh: “我们也想招收新成员，但不幸的是*当前的学术环境*十分排斥亚唯物主义思想。这年头，如果你是左派，主流学派就只有戈特伍德和经济眼了。”

En: Don't forget about Maurice and the turnips.
Zh: “别忘了莫里斯和萝卜的事情。”

En: He sighs. "Right, then there was the whole 'turnip' debacle."
Zh: 他叹了口气。“对，然后又是这个‘萝卜’大失败。”

En: Whatever this turnip business is about, one thing *is* perfectly clear: These young students have a much deeper understanding of communism than you do...
Zh: 不管这个萝卜是什么东西，有一件事情*是*很清楚的：这些年轻学生对康米主义的理解，可比你深得多了……

En: You could learn a thing or two from them, if you can convince them you're one of them.
Zh: 如果你让他们相信你也是其中一员，或许就能从他们身上学到点什么。

En: They're the most depressing school of communism. They love writing long books with a patina of Mazovian theory to cover up their cheap psychologising.
Zh: “他们是康米主义最丧气的一个学派，净喜欢大书特书廉价的心理学分析，然后点缀一些马佐夫主义理论作为掩饰。”

En: It's okay for Uli to say that because his dad is from Gottwald.
Zh: “尤利的爸爸就是戈特伍德学派的成员，所以他这么说没关系。”

En: What's so depressing about their theories?
Zh: “他们的理论是怎么个丧气法？”

En: What's so bad about psychologising?
Zh: “心理学分析哪里不好了？”

En: The Gottwald School believe that intellectuals as a class are incapable of sparking revolutionary change, so all they can do is *critique* capitalism from inside itself.
Zh: “戈特伍德学派相信知识分子阶级无法推动真正的社会革命，只能在资本主义的内部*批判*它……”

En: That sounds miserable.
Zh: “听起来很可悲。”

En: Sounds rewarding, actually.
Zh: “其实还挺有意义的。”

En: It *is* miserable. That's probably why they're always committing suicide.
Zh: “它*确实*很可悲。这就是为什么他们总在自杀……”

En: If it were, they wouldn't keep committing suicide.
Zh: “如果这种生活真的有意义，他们就不会自杀了……”

En: Do you think all the problems in the world can be reduced to repressed sexual urges?
Zh: “你觉得世界上所有的问题都可以归咎于性压抑吗？”

En: Speaking from experience, definitely.
Zh: “以我的经验之谈，当然如此。”

En: Probably not.
Zh: “应该不行吧。”

En: I can tell you're a man who has his urges, but I can also tell you're no Gottwaldian. He smirks.
Zh: 他冷笑。“你一看就是个性欲高涨的男人，但是我也能看出来你不是戈特伍德派……”

En: Well, there you have it. You're not a Gottwaldian, then.
Zh: “那就对了。你就不是戈特伍德派……”

En: You see, the Gottwald School *look* like communists, they *talk* like communists, but scratch the patina and you'll see beneath that they're just depressed liberals who've read too many books.
Zh: “你瞧，戈特伍德学派*看起来*就像康米主义，*讲话*也像康米主义，但剥掉他们那层外皮，底下不过是一群读了太多书的丧气的自由主义者。”

En: That's why they spend all their time smoking cigarettes and writing long works of criticism that make you want to commit suicide.
Zh: “这就为什么他们成天抽雪茄，长篇累牍地写那些看了让人想自杀的批判文学。”

En: For starters, they love talking about *beans*.
Zh: “比方说，他们热衷于讨论*豆子*。”

En: *Beans*?
What's wrong with beans? I like beans.
Zh: “豆子怎么了？我喜欢豆子。”

En: No beans for me. Can't stand the stuff.
Zh: “不要豆子。受不了这东西。”

En: That's right. Econoclards are *obsessed* with beans. They love thinking about beans, they love counting beans, but most of all, they love building models to predict how many beans there'll be *in the future*.
Zh: “对，豆子。经济眼们*痴迷*于豆子。他们喜欢想豆子，数豆子，还有最重要的，构建模型来预测*以后*还会长出多少豆子……”

En: Oh, sure. But Econoclards are *obsessed* with beans. They love thinking about beans, they love counting beans, but most of all, they love building models to predict how many beans there'll be *in the future*.
Zh: “这没关系。但是经济眼们*痴迷*于豆子。他们喜欢想豆子，数豆子，还有最重要的，构建模型来预测*以后*还会长出多少豆子……”

En: You're no Econoclard, then. They're *obsessed* with beans. They love thinking about beans, they love counting beans, but most of all, they love building models to predict how many beans there'll be *in the future*.
Zh: “那你就不是经济眼了。他们*痴迷*于豆子。他们喜欢想豆子，数豆子，还有构建模型来模拟*以后*还会长出多少豆子……”

En: Nota bene: 'Econoclard' is an extra-pejorative form of the already pejorative name 'Mazovian Economists', a moderate school of Mazovianism, which advocates a gradual transition to communism through carefully managed economic modernisation rather than violent social revolution.
Zh: 注意：“马佐夫主义经济学家”本身就是一种蔑称，“经济眼”则是比那更鄙夷的超级-蔑称。这个温和的马佐夫主义学派主张通过精心操作的经济现代化，而非暴力的社会革命，使社会形态逐渐过渡到康米主义。

En: They're by far the most bean-centric school of communism.
Zh: “他们是目前为止最豆子中心主义的康米主义学派。”

En: Ah yes, the much maligned bean counters, ensconced in their think-tanks and highrises, believing they can save the world through a series of incremental, assiduously technocratic reforms...
Zh: 啊，饱受中伤的数豆人，缩在他们的思维坦克与摩天大楼里，坚信他们能够通过一系列渐进式、专家设计的，勤勤恳恳的改良措施来改变世界……

En: I don't get it. Are the beans a *metaphor*?
Zh: “我没听懂。豆子是什么*隐喻*吗？”

En: But isn't it good to know how many beans there are?
Zh: “知道以后会有多少豆子难道不好吗？”

En: What's wrong with making progress through moderate economic reforms?
Zh: “温和的经济改革有什么不对？”

En: If only! They've got all the beans accounted for in their asset sheets, their quarterly budgets, their future projections. But for some reason there are never enough beans to go around, so we've just got to cut our bean rations in half...
Zh: “要真是这样就好了！他们已经把所有的豆子，他们的季度预算和预测目标，统统填进了资产负债表。但是不知为何，豆子总是不够，所以我们的豆子配给又得减半……”

En: See? You're already falling for that scarcity logic. You start off *assuming* there aren't enough beans to go around, so naturally you want to cut the bean rations in half...
Zh: “你瞧？你已经被那个稀缺性的逻辑绕进去了。你一开始就*假定*豆子不够多，所以你自然会想把豆子的配给减半……”

En: Oh, please. There's nothing 'moderate' about their love for counting beans. If I were a Gottwaldian, I would probably diagnose them with *Bohnenfixierung*...
Zh: “哦，拜托。他们对数豆子的热爱没有一点*温和*的地方。如果我是戈特伍德派，我可能会诊断他们患有*豆子固着*……”

En: You see, Econoclards claim to be communists, but in reality they're just liberals with hard-ons for spreadsheets.
Zh: “你瞧，经济眼自称是康米主义者，但实际上不过是一群会对着报表硬起来的自由主义者。”

En: Of course not. The only people who actually call themselves liberals are mouth-foaming reactionaries.
Zh: “当然不是。只有破口大骂的反动派才会自称自由主义者。”

En: Basically indistinguishable from fascists. You'd need an x-ray machine to tell the difference.
Zh: “和法西斯主义者基本一致。你得拿X光机才能照出来区别。”

En: Another sigh. “It's an unfortunate story. You see, our ex-comrade Maurice is something of an economist...”
Zh: 又一声叹息。“这是个不幸的故事。你瞧，我们的前-同志莫里斯可以算是个经济学家……”

En: “He’s studying macro- *and* micro-economics.”
Zh: “他正在学习宏观*和*微观经济。”

En: Wow, a *real* intellectual, it sounds like. The lieutenant arches his eyebrows.
Zh: “哇哦，听起来*真*是一位学者。”警督扬起眉毛。

En: “Right, so a few weeks ago we were discussing the extra-physical capabilities of the revolutionary state, and Maurice said... what were his exact words, Ulixes?"
Zh: “是的，几周前我们在讨论革命国家的超物理能力，然后莫里斯说……尤利西斯，他具体说了什么来着？”

En: It was unbelievable. He said, 'Turnips don't care if they're grown by communists, moralists, or welkin. They grow just the same'.
Zh: “我不敢相信。他居然说，‘萝卜才不在乎种它的人是康米主义者，道德家，还是精灵。它们长起来都一样。’”

En: Just a minute. Steban... Ulixes... *why* do those names ring the faintest of bells?
Zh: 等一下。史蒂文……尤利西斯……*为什么*这些名字给你一种隐隐的熟悉感？

En: Probably because they're the real names of 'Nasteb' and 'Exilus', the authors of that so-called essay about TipTop Tournée you read in *La Fumée*.
Zh: 可能因为它们就是‘凡史蒂’和‘西利尤斯’的真名，也就是你之前在*化为轻烟*上读过的那篇所谓的大师巡回赛批判的作者。

En: You should get to the bottom of this, when you have the chance.
Zh: 你应该抽时间仔细读一读那篇文章。

En: Basically, he was rejecting the whole foundation of infra-materialist theory.
Zh: “基本上来说，他否认了亚唯物主义的整个理论基础。”

En: What is this *infra-materialist* business they keep blathering about? You've never heard of anything like it.
Zh: 他们一直滔滔不绝的这个*亚唯物主义*到底是个什么东西？你从来没听说过它。

En: To be fair, the theory *is* pretty out there.
Zh: “公道地说，这个理论*确实*很离奇。”

En: Man, what an ignoramus. (Shake your head.)
Zh: “老天，真是一个愚人。”（摇摇头。）

En: Remind me what infra-materialist theory says about turnips again?
Zh: “我想不起来亚唯物主义和萝卜有什么关系了，你能提醒我一下吗？”

En: Just go along with it.
Zh: 顺着他的话说。

En: We're extremely disappointed. Maurice used to be a good friend of ours.
Zh: “我们大失所望。莫里斯曾经是我们好朋友。”

En: I'll grant you it can be overwhelming at first, but once you understand the basics, it's all quite intuitive...
Zh: “我向你保证，虽然它一开始令人云里雾里，但只要你理解了理论的基础，剩下的内容就都很直观了……”

En: Simply that *under suitably revolutionary conditions* crop yields naturally increase relative to non-revolutionary crops. Which Maurice somehow has the gall to deny.
Zh: “简单地说，在*在某些适当的革命环境中*，作物的产量会高于不革命的作物。莫里斯竟有胆量否认这一点……”

En: Zuzanna said that he has been hanging out with some non-communists lately.
Zh: “祖扎娜说他最近在和一些非康米主义者来往。”

En: Providing you haven't been hanging out with non-communists, like Maurice.
Zh: “前提是你没有像莫里斯那样，和非康米主义者混在一起。”

En: For us the question boiled down to: 'If you don't even accept the basic ideas of Nilsen and infra-materialist theory, why are you in the reading group?'
Zh: “于是我们的问题变成了：‘如果你甚至不能接受尼尔森和亚唯物主义的理论基础，那你为什么还要参加这个阅读小组？’”

En: Right, and this Nilsen is...?
Zh: “没错，这个尼尔森又是……？”

En: I totally understand.
Zh: “我完全理解。”

En: I don't understand at all.
Zh: “我什么都没听懂。”

En: So you expelled Maurice from the reading group over an argument about turnips?
Zh: “所以你们因为一个和萝卜有关的争论，就把莫里斯从阅读小组里赶出去了？”

En: Nilsen. Ignus Nilsen.
Zh: “尼尔森。伊格纳斯·尼尔森。”

En: Of course you do. It's pretty basic stuff!
Zh: “你应该理解。这是最基础的东西！”

En: Exactly. What educated person could believe that turnips grow at the same rate under capitalism *and* communism?
Zh: “一点没错。受过教育的人怎么能相信，萝卜在资本主义*和*康米主义之下，会有相同的生长速率？”

En: Well, it wasn't so much that he was expelled...
Zh: “这个嘛，倒不是说我们把他赶走了……”

En: It's a sad reflection on our educational institutions.
Zh: “我们的教育机构是多么可悲啊。”

En: He just quit coming. We haven't seen him around for weeks.
Zh: “是他自己不来了。我们好几周没看到过他了。”

En: What were you doing with those matchboxes just now?
Zh: “这些火柴盒又是怎么回事？”

En: The young man frowns at the little pile of boxes on the floor.
Zh: 年轻人对着地上的一小摊火柴盒皱起眉头。

En: Nothing, just messing around until the meeting started.
Zh: “没什么，只是在集会开始前随便玩玩。”

En: They're watching those matchboxes awfully intently for two guys who are just 'messing around.'
Zh: 他们紧张兮兮地看着这些火柴盒。这可不像是‘随便玩玩’的态度。

En: It's almost as though they were trying to create the most unstable structure they could...
Zh: 看起来好像他们试图用火柴盒搭建出一个极其不稳定的结构……

En: With predictable results.
Zh: 结果可想而知。

En: You two wrote that article in La Fumée, didn't you?
Zh: “等等，你们两个写了化为轻烟上的那篇文章，对吧？”

En: Do I *know* you two from somewhere?
Zh: “等等，我是不是在哪里认识过你们？”

En: *You've* read our article? For the first time since you've met the young man, words seem to desert him...
Zh: “*你看过*我们的文章？”从你们见面以来，年轻人似乎第一次失语了。

En: I don't think so.  The young man gives you a curious look. "Unless you've been hanging around the Cultural Studies faculty at the École Normale de Revachol..."
Zh: 年轻人好奇地看你一眼。“我不这么觉得，除非你曾经来瑞瓦肖高师的文化研究系转悠过……”

En: That I did not expect. His companion is blushing now, a sheepish grin on his face.
Zh: “这我是真没想到。”他的同伴已经脸红起来，露出一点羞怯的微笑。

En: The energy in the room has shifted ever so slightly in your favour. They're afraid they've somehow *embarrassed* themselves in front of you.
Zh: 房间内的局势似乎微妙地向你倾斜了。他们开始担心自己会在你面前*露怯*……

En: Well, don't keep us on tenterhooks! What did you think of the essay?
Zh: “别吊胃口了！你觉得文章写得怎么样？”

En: The delicate egos on these boys! Even though you're just some cop they're desperate for your approval.
Zh: 男孩子脆弱的自尊心！连你这个条子的认可都那么在意。

En: Hey! You're not just 'some cop', you've got highly developed critical faculties! Now's your chance to show them off.
Zh: 嘿！你可不是‘普通的条子’，你有着高度发达的学术批判能力！是时候在他们面前露一手了。

En: Time to meet these two on the intellectual battlefield. Why don't you show off those highly developed critical faculties of yours?
Zh: 到了在学术战场上和他们两个短兵相接的时候了。何不向他们露一手你那高度发达的学术批判能力？

En: (Show them your edits.) "I actually had a few pieces of constructive criticism..."
Zh: （向他们展示你的修订。）“我恰好有一些建设性意见……”

En: To be honest, it doesn't sound like you know anything about motor racing.
Zh: “老实说，我觉得你们好像对赛車一窍不通。”

En: It sounded like a recycled university essay, frankly.
Zh: “坦白说，你们的文章看起来像一篇被导师打回去的论文初稿。”

En: I liked the parts about death and dismemberment. That's what racing's all about.
Zh: “我喜欢你们写死亡和肢解的部分。这就是赛車的看点。”

En: It was a good article. You should keep developing your ideas.
Zh: “是一篇好文章。你们应该继续深入分析。”

En: The young man takes the folded up magazine with some hesitation. As he reads over your annotations and emendations a look of deep concentration comes over his face...
Zh: 年轻人有些犹豫地接过那本折起的杂志。他读着你的注释与校订，逐渐全神贯注起来……

En: Well, I hope you'll let us know when *your* monograph is coming out...
Zh: “好吧，那什么时候*你的*大作发表了，记得告诉我们……”

En: Well, yes, that's the crux of our thesis. Ulixes is especially interested in capitalist violence.
Zh: “是的，没错，这确实是我们的核心论点。尤利西斯对资本主义暴力特别感兴趣。”

En: Well, of course that's just an *initial foray* into the subject. We're hoping to return to it for a more substantial treatment next term...
Zh: “好吧，那当然只是我们针对这个主题的*首次尝试*。下学期我们会把它写得更加扎实……”

En: In any case, I'm glad our piece found its audience. That's always the hope with these things, you know.
Zh: “不管怎么说，我很高兴我们的文章有了观众。我们写文章就是为了这个，你知道的。”

En: I bet he's never written a substantial essay himself. He's really got no right to critique. None at all.
Zh: “我猜他从来没正经写过一篇论文。他没有权利批判我们的文章，完全没有。”

En: He has the look of a man spread naked on the operating table. He can feel the cool metal of the scalpel, the bracing sting of the antiseptic, and yet his expression isn't wholly unpleasant. A part of him actually seems to *enjoy* the attention.
Zh: 他看起来就像一个赤裸着躺在手术台上的男人。他能感觉到手术刀的冰冷刀锋，和消毒药水带来的锐痛，但他的表情却并非全然痛苦。他似乎隐隐地*享受*着这等关注。

En: What does it say, Steban? I can't see.
Zh: “上面写了什么，史蒂文？我看不清。”

En: Well, there you did it. You stung them, and to what end?
Zh: 哈，你做到了。你把他们都震住了。为了什么呢？

En: They're... good notes. A little brutal on questions of style, but he's not *wrong* about any of it...
Zh: “这些修订……写得很好。提问的风格略显粗犷，但这些问题并没有任何*错误*的地方……”

En: Would it be alright if I kept this for a while? I'd just like some time to think about your comments. He turns to you.
Zh: “我可以把它借走一段时间吗？我想更仔细地思考你的评论。”他转向你。

En: Sure, you hang onto it.
Zh: “当然，拿着吧。”

En: No, that's my only copy.
Zh: “不行，我只有这本。”

En: Thanks, I appreciate it.
Zh: “谢谢，我很感激。”

En: Sure, right. I got the main idea anyway.
Zh: “好吧，没事。反正我已经记住你的主旨思想了。”

En: Really just violence in general. I find it *fascinating*.
Zh: “说到底就是暴力。我觉得它*很有趣*。”

En: Something about this one makes you want to avoid dark alleys at night...
Zh: 不知为何，这个人让你不想一个人走夜路了……

En: Perhaps he subscribes to 'La Fumée'.
Zh: “也许他订阅了‘化为轻烟’。”

En: That's sarcasm. He does not expect you to subscribe to radical communist periodicals.
Zh: 这是讽刺。他没指望你订阅激进康米主义期刊。

En: Of course! They're the ones who produced that overwrought *critique* of TipTop Tournée.
Zh: 当然！他们可是那篇超级亢奋的大师巡回赛*批判*的作者。

En: Aha, you two wrote the piece about motor carriage racing.
Zh: “啊哈，就是你们两个写了那篇赛車的文章。”

En: (Show them 'La Fumée'.) "Wait, *you* guys wrote for *this*?"
Zh: （向他们展示‘化为轻烟’。）“等等，*你们*两个给*它*供稿？”

En: Is the reading group accepting new members?
Zh: “阅读小组还招人吗？”

En: We typically only accept new members once per semester. There's this whole *process*, with essays and presentations on assigned topics... The young man turns to his companion.
Zh: “我们通常每学期只招新一次。我们有一整套*流程*，还要写论文，对选定主题的演示……”年轻人转向他的同伴。

En: But given that we have some extra seating at the moment, I guess we could be convinced to expedite an application or two.
Zh: “但考虑到目前我们有多余的名额，我想我们可以接收一到二名申请者。”

En: Steban, you can't be serious... for these gendarmes?\n
Zh: “史蒂文，你不会是认真地要接收……这些警官吧？”

En: Steban, you can't be serious... for a gendarme?
Zh: “史蒂文，你不会是认真地要接收……这位警官吧？”

En: I am serious. As materialists we've got to adapt to conditions as they are. Besides, he'll still need to pass the *interview portion* of the entrance process... He turns back to you.
Zh: “我是认真的。作为唯物主义者，我们需要随机应变。再说，他还得通过招新流程的*面试部分*呢……”他又转回到你面前。

En: ... assuming he's even still interested, that is.
Zh: “……当然，如果他感兴趣的话。”

En: Convince them you belong in the reading group.
Zh: 说服他们你应当成为阅读小组的一员。

En: Sub-literate nematode.
Zh: 亚文学线虫。

En: Harmless book slug.
Zh: 无害的书本蛞蝓。

En: Somewhat bookish toad.
Zh: 掉书袋的蟾蜍。

En: Proud bookshop ruminant.
Zh: 骄傲的书店反刍动物。

En: Voracious biblophilic teratorn.
Zh: 贪婪嗜书巨鸟。

En: UNSTOPPABLE LITERARY WÜRM.
Zh: 无尽文学巨虫

En: Standard Wirrâl Die.
Zh: 标准威勒尔骰子

En: What's there to be scared of? You've really been cracking the books these last few days. You can go toe-to-intellectual-toe with any reading group in Martinaise...
Zh: 有什么可怕的？这几天你可是博览群书。不管去马丁内斯的哪个阅读小组，你都能进行一番紧张刺激的学术对话……

En: Take a deep breath. You've done some reading these past few days. But you also know there's more to life than what you find in books...
Zh: 深呼吸。这几天你读了一些书。但你知道生命的意义不仅在于书本……

En: Remember: Where most people go wrong is in thinking they have to hide what they don't know...
Zh: 记住：大部分人不懂装懂的时候是会出问题的……

En: You've spent a not inconsiderable amount of time arranging the works in your mental library by theme and period. All the ideas and references you'll need are ready-at-hand.
Zh: 你在自己的脑内图书馆里花了不少时间按照主题和时期整理书籍作品。任何你需要的论点和参考资料都已准备就绪。

En: You've survived things these kids can't even imagine. They've got nothing on you.
Zh: 你经历过的事情这些小朋友想都想不到。他们对你一无所知。

En: Another thing, these young men have the most exquisitely tuned insecurity receptors you've ever witnessed.
Zh: 何况，这两个年轻人是你见过最容易不安的人。

En: Now, chin up. You've got this.
Zh: 现在，昂首挺胸，你能做到的。

En: This is just the group I've been looking for. When do we start?
Zh: “我就想加入你们这样的小组。我们什么时候开始？”

En: (Crack your knuckles.) "Alright, let's do this interview."
Zh: （掰指节。）“好极了，让我们开始这场面试吧。”

En: Oh, you want to start *now*? He looks to his companion, then back at you. "Sure, we can manage that..."
Zh: “哦，你*现在*就想开始？”他看看同伴，然后视线转回到你。“当然，那我们来吧……”

En: You've caught him off-balance. The momentum is already in your favour.
Zh: 你打了他一个措手不及。局势对你有利。

En: Go ahead and take a seat. Since we haven't had time to prepare an exhaustive questionnaire, I think we can keep this interview more freeform.
Zh: “过来坐下。我们没来得及准备一份详细的问卷，所以我想用更随意一些的方式进行面试。”

En: Why don't you tell us a bit about the books you're interested in, and we'll just see where the conversation goes.
Zh: “不如你和我们说说你感兴趣的书本，然后我们顺其自然。”

En: Like you guys, I really get into political and historical subjects.
Zh: “和你们一样，我热衷于政治和历史书籍。”

En: For me, it's more about finding the right thing for the right moment.
Zh: “对我来说，在正确的时间找到正确的事情是最重要的。”

En: I'm more into practical books. Books that help me understand the real world.
Zh: “我更偏好实用书籍。能帮助我理解现实世界的那些。”

En: Look guys, I've gotta be honest. I may not have 'read' many 'books', but that doesn't mean I don't have *stories*.
Zh: “伙计们，我得说老实话。我‘读’过的‘书籍’并不多，但这不代表着我就不是一个有*故事*的男人。”

En: ... don't you think, though, that you can only really understand current events if you have a firm background in history and political theory?
Zh: “……但你难道不觉得，你得建立起对历史和政治理论的坚实基础，才能真正理解身边发生的事件吗？”

En: Absolutely. It's the only proper foundation to develop a real understanding of events. He nods in vigorous agreement.
Zh: “一点没错。只有读这些才能让你真正理解身边发生的事件。”他激烈地点头表示同意。

En: There might be something to that. I recently finished 'The Greatest Innocence'.
Zh: “或许有点道理。我最近刚读完‘最伟大的无罪者’。”

En: That reminds me, Kim and I just played this interesting game, 'Suzerainty'.
Zh: “这让我想到，我和金最近刚玩了一个有趣的游戏‘大领主’。”

En: But once you've got a political lens, you can read anything critically. Even those Hjelmdallermann books.
Zh: “但只要你拥有了政治化的视角，你就可以批判性地阅读任何材料。即使是那些赫姆达尔英雄传丛书。”

En: Listen, books are fine and all, but to be straight with you, they're no substitute for a real story.
Zh: “听着，书籍很好，但是和你们实话实话，没有东西能代替一个真正的故事。”

En: Hmm, don't think I'm familiar with it. Give us a quick summary, if you don't mind.
Zh: “哼……我好像对它并不熟悉。如果不介意的话，请你简单介绍一番。”

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. You quickly gloss Lopez de Fuego's essential argument, peppering it with your own commentary and asides...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。你快速地解读了洛佩斯·富埃戈的核心论点，并附以你本人的评价与见解……

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. You quickly gloss Lopez de Fuego's essential argument, peppering it with your own commentary and asides...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中，于是快速地解读了洛佩斯·富埃戈的核心论点，并附以你本人的评价与见解……

En: Like a river emptying into the sea, the discussion winds its way toward the character of the innocence herself.
Zh: 如同河水奔流入海，你们的讨论话题转向了无罪者本人的品格。

En: ... but then, any *critical* account of Dolores Dei's reign has to seriously reckon with her atrocities in Magritania and La Vuelta during the Mesque secession, don't you think?
Zh: “……但是，任何对于德洛莉丝·黛的统治的*批评*，都离不开她在梅斯克独立时期在马格利塔尼亚与拉布埃尔塔的暴行，你难道不觉得吗？”

En: Lopez de Fuego seemed to think she was pretty great regardless.
Zh: “但洛佩斯·富埃戈似乎依然将她视为一位伟人。”

En: Honestly, I did get the impression there was something inhuman about her.
Zh: “老实说，我确实感觉她有些非人类的地方。”

En: Exactly. Lopez de Fuego totally glosses over all her mass murder.
Zh: “一点不错。洛佩斯·富埃戈热衷于粉饰甚至鼓吹她所有的大屠杀罪行。”

En: I'm a little rusty on my innocentic history, so it was helpful for me.
Zh: “我对无罪历史有点记忆模糊了，所以这本书对我来说挺有帮助的。”

En: Total nonsense. The greatest thing Dolores Dei ever did was get blasted by her own therrier.
Zh: “一派胡言。德洛莉丝·黛这辈子只做过一件好事，那就是被她自己的梗犬打死。”

En: The whole innocentic system practically requires that. Without true gods we're made to set them up for ourselves. He nods vigorously.
Zh: “整个无罪体系就建立在这之上。没有真正的神灵，我们就得自己立起神像。”他激烈地点头表示同意。

En: Ha! It's good you were able to see through even that gauzy nostalgic pulp.
Zh: “哈！你看那么轻薄感伤的通俗书籍都能想到这一点，可真不错。”

En: I'll say that beginnings are very delicate things. That makes it all the more important that you start with *the right books*.
Zh: “我得说开头的印象是很精密的东西。所以说从*正确的书籍*开始就更加重要了。”

En: If he wants a real critical history, he's got to read 'On the Material Conditions of the Mirovan Boilermakers'.
Zh: “如果他想读真正的批判历史，他得读‘米洛凡锅炉厂鸡尾酒的物质条件’。”

En: It's so good. Brutally revelatory.
Zh: “它写得非常好。对现实的揭露堪称一针见血。”

En: Oh, right. We've been meaning to try that out.
Zh: “哦对。我们一直想试试它。”

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. Soon you're engaged in a thoughtful back-and-forth about the relationship between theme and mechanics...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。很快你又同他们谈起文学创作中的主题思想与表现手法之间的关系，并发表了一番深邃的批判……

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. Soon you're engaged in a thoughtful back-and-forth about the relationship between theme and mechanics...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。很快你又同他们谈起文学创作中的主题思想与表现手法之间的关系，并发表了一番深邃的批判……

En: Best of all, your arguments are liberally seasoned with references to particular historical events and personages.
Zh: 最妙的是，你的论述富有文采地引用了特定历史事件与人物。

En: ... but then my issue with these historical cube-pushers is that they offer the *illusion* of subjectivity even as they reinscribe the logic of colonialism itself...
Zh: “……但在我看来，这些历史大富翁游戏的问题在于，他们提供了一种*虚假的*主观性，然而实际上却只是在重复殖民主义的逻辑……”

En: ... though I'd love to hear your take on it.
Zh: “……不过我乐于倾听你对它的看法。”

En: For me, games are mostly about spending time with a partner.
Zh: “对我来说，游戏最重要的是和搭档一起玩。”

En: I thought it was pretty fun. No need to intellectualise it too much.
Zh: “我觉得它很有趣。不用对它太上纲上线。”

En: But by allowing players to *recreate* historical events, like the introduction of cocaine to Safre, doesn't it force them to question what 'subjectivity' means in a ludo-thematic context?
Zh: “但它允许玩家*重新构建*历史事件，比如可卡因流入萨弗里的始末，这难道不会迫使他们思考，在游戏语境下的‘主观性’到底意味着什么吗？”

En: Hmm. What is it they say about ties? That they're like kissing your sister... or in this case, I suppose, your partner.
Zh: “哼……他们说的这个平局是什么？说什么他们像在亲吻你的妹妹……或者在这种情况下，我猜，你的搭档。”

En: He's only saying that because he got the better of me, somehow...
Zh: “他这么说，只是因为他不知怎么赢过了我……”

En: He's only saying that because he *likes* getting thrashed like a schoolboy.
Zh: “他这么说，只是因为他*喜欢*像个学生一样被痛击。”

En: Yes, and it only took about four hours of our lives that we'll never get back.
Zh: “是的，它只花了我们人生中再也回不来的短短四个小时。”

En: Listen, this is a forward-thinking group. It's entirely your business, of course.
Zh: “注意，我们的阅读小组是富有创新精神的。当然，这完全是你自己的决定。”

En: With his 'partner', he says.
Zh: “和他的‘搭档’一起。他说。”

En: Anyway, we also enjoy a good board game every once in a while. The other week Ulixes and I tried this new one where everyone is competing to manage their own Zone of Ecological Catastrophe. You should see the components.
Zh: “不管怎么说，我们有时也会享受优秀的桌游。前几周尤利西斯和我试了一个新游戏，所有玩家经营自己的生态灾难地区并彼此竞争。你能看到它的组成部分。”

En: Of course. The one thing capitalism does better than every other ideology is novel and clever distractions. It's all one never-ending dopamine dripper.
Zh: “当然。资本主义在创造新奇精巧的消遣活动这一点上，做得比其他任何意识形态都更好。永不停歇的多巴胺滴嘴。”

En: Capitalism *rules*, is what he seems to be saying.
Zh: 我看他的意思就是，资本主义*牛逼*。

En: Drip after drip after drip after drip... The young man's companion appears to be mashing his fist into his palm.
Zh: “一滴又一滴又一滴又一滴……”年轻人的同伴捶了一下自己的掌心。

En: Interesting. Say more about that...
Zh: “有趣。再多说点……”

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. Soon you're in a lively back and forth on the political substrata of the Hjelmdallermann universe...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。很快你又同他们兴致勃勃地谈起赫姆达尔宇宙的政治大环境……

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. Soon you're in a lively back and forth on the political substrata of the Hjelmdallermann universe...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。很快你又同他们兴致勃勃地谈起赫姆达尔宇宙的政治潜台词……

En: Even if they won't admit it, these two love blood and sex as much as anyone.
Zh: 这两人和其他人一样喜欢血腥和性，即使他们并不愿意承认。

En: ... but don't you think that pornographic fascist mythology gets pretty tiresome after a few books?
Zh: “……但你难道不觉得，那套色情法西斯神话丛书越看越累？”

En: He nods to your shirt. "No offence, of course."
Zh: 他对你的主题T恤点点头：“当然，我没有冒犯你的意思。”

En: Let them know your interest in muscleman literature is much more high-minded than they're giving you credit for.
Zh: 让他们知道，你对肌肉男文学的兴趣比他们愿意承认得要高雅得多。

En: Oh, certainement. I'm more interested in them as cultural artifacts.
Zh: “哦，当然。我对它们感兴趣是把它们当成文化制品来看。”

En: No way, I live for that kind of stuff.
Zh: “怎么可能。我就爱这种东西。”

En: Nah, it's just good escapist fun.
Zh: “那倒没有，它用来打发时间还不错。”

En: Ah, well there's certainly value in that kind of reading. Comrade Mazov says something on that note in his collected speeches...
Zh: “啊，这种阅读当然还是有价值的。马佐夫同志在他的演讲中评论过这点……”

En: Ha! Of course you do. I just hope when you're reading it you've got a sturdy pair of gloves on...
Zh: “哈！就知道你会这样。我只希望你在读的时候戴上一双厚实的手套……”

En: Oh, sure, we don't mean to deny the value of *pleasure* in reading.
Zh: “哦，当然，我们没有想要否认阅读的*乐趣*。”

En: 'To find victory in the cultural war we must first scout the battlefield'.
Zh: “为了赢下这场文化战争，我们必须首先侦察战场。”

En: Can't let any of the dreck rub off on you.
Zh: “不能蹭到那些垃圾。”

En: In any case, I think we've heard enough. We could use someone with your perspective in the group. With just a bit more theoretical foundation, I think you'll be making real contributions.
Zh: “我想我们听得够多了。你的视角将会对我们的小组很有帮助。只要再多一些理论基础，我相信你会作出杰出的贡献。”

En: These kids are eating out of your hand, practically. Another quarter of an hour disappears. The questions come rapid-fire, but you have an answer for every one.
Zh: 这两个孩子简直字面意义上地抱着你不放。时间又流逝了十五分钟。他们像机关枪一样急切地询问你，但你答上了每个问题。

En: Now, you can sense things starting to slow down. The interview must be reaching an inflection point...
Zh: 现在，你能感觉到对话的节奏逐渐放慢。面试一定到了转折点……

En: We prefer difficult books. Books that might destroy you, if you don't destroy them first.
Zh: “我们更喜欢艰难的书籍。要么你摧毁它们，要么就被它们摧毁。”

En: That's enough, Uli. It's the gendarme's opinions we're interested in.
Zh: “这些就够了，尤利。我们只需要知道警官的观点。”

En: ... but to Comrade Ulixes' point, isn't there value in reading really difficult books, just for the sheer challenge of it?
Zh: “……但说到尤利西斯同志的问题，难道阅读艰难的书籍本身就没有价值吗，这不是一个很好的挑战吗？”

En: I tried reading this extremely depressing Graadian novel.
Zh: “我试图阅读这本极其悲苦的格拉德长篇小说。”

En: To be honest, these days I prefer escapist stuff, like classic Dick Mullen.
Zh: “老实说，这年头我只想看点轻松的消遣书籍了，就像经典的迪克马伦系列。”

En: I'm more into works of pure imagination, like 'Wirrâl'.
Zh: “我更喜欢纯粹的幻想书籍，比如‘威勒尔’系列。”

En: Listen, books are fine and all, but to be straight with you, they're no substitute for a real story.
Zh: “听着，书籍很好，但是和你们实话实话，没有东西能代替一个真正的故事。”

En: Oh, right. 'Sixteen Days of Coldest... Whatever'. What was your take on it?
Zh: “哦对，‘最冷的那16天’……大概是这个名字。你对它是什么看法？”

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. You ably summarise the novel's characters and themes...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。你轻松自如地概述了这本长篇小说的角色与主题……

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. You ably summarise the novel's characters and themes...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。你轻松自如地概述了这本长篇小说的角色与主题……

En: Even better, you're able to connect those themes to your critique of the novel's formal qualities, such as they are.
Zh: 更妙的是，你把这些主题同你对小说的叙事结构批判结合在了一起。

En: ... so anyway I had to give up. There was no point in subjecting myself to needless suffering.
Zh: “所以最后我放弃了。没有必要自找苦吃。”

En: ... but eventually I finished it, however much physical pain it caused me.
Zh: “……但最终我忍受着无边的苦痛读完了它。”

En: It's no wonder you couldn't finish it. Sounds like turgid bourgeois social realism. He nods approvingly.
Zh: “难怪你没有读完。听起来像是浮夸的资产阶级社会现实主义作品。”他点头赞同。

En: Sounds to me like a typical bourgeois social novel, designed to assuage the egos of frustrated housewives and win international awards.
Zh: “听起来像是典型的资产阶级世情小说，写出来的目的就是向沮丧的家庭主妇提供安慰，并且在国际上刷奖。”

En: And Graadian realism is the worst realism. It's nearly as bad as Gottwaldian critical theory.
Zh: “而且格拉德现实主义又是最烂的现实主义。简直和戈特伍德派的批判理论不相上下。”

En: Makes you want to gouge your eyes out. Or better, gouge the author's eyes out.
Zh: “让你想把自己的眼睛挖出来。或者更好，把作者的眼睛挖出来。”

En: I'm sure as a gendarme you have a *very* interesting take on the Mullen character.
Zh: “我相信警官你肯定对马伦的角色塑造有着*非常*有趣的见解。”

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. This is your area of expertise, after all...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。毕竟，这可是你的专业领域……

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. This is your area of expertise, after all...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。毕竟，这可是你的专业领域……

En: That's right. These two young men naturally defer to your aura of authority, which you nevertheless wear as lightly as your halogen watermarks.
Zh: 没错。这两个年轻人天然地屈从于你的权威。不管如今处于怎样的状态，这种权威的光晕依然像卤素水印一样隐隐可见。

En: ... but in the end Mullen is a reflection of a politico-legal institution that's irreconcilably divided between protecting 'the people' and defending the material interests of capital.
Zh: “……但说到底马伦只是一个政治-法律机构的倒影，而这个机构在保护‘人民’与守卫资本的现实利益这两个职能之间，存在着无法调和的矛盾。”

En: ... but really I just like him because I'm a cop too, you know?
Zh: “……但我真的只是因为同为条子而喜欢他，你们明白吗？”

En: ... but then the whole book fell apart before I could solve the mystery...
Zh: “……但在我能够解答谜题之前，整本书的逻辑已经分崩离析……”

En: The lieutenant doesn't speak, but you believe you detect a slight widening of his eyes, as though he never expected such a cogent thought to emerge from your mouth.
Zh: 警督什么也没说，但你注意到他的双眼微微睁大，好像没有预料到你会说出这样掷地有声的话语。

En: A compelling point, for sure. We'd probably be better off if more police officers learned to think so reflectively about their social function.
Zh: “这确实是一个强有力的论点。如果更多警官能够学会这样批判性地思考他们的职能，我们的社会可就好得多啦。”

En: Makes sense. It's natural to seek out reflections of your own experience in literature when the politico-economic system doesn't permit genuine self-realization.
Zh: “合情合理。在政治-经济体系不允许你真正视线自我价值的时候，从文学中寻找自己经历的倒影是很正常的。”

En: That's an unfortunate quality of pulp fiction. Much of it is written to be disposable both on an intellectual *and* a material level.
Zh: “不幸的是，通俗小说常常是这样的。不管是从内容*还是*装帧的角度来看，大多数这类小说都是让人看完就扔的。”

En: But at least crime literature *as a genre* is able to engage with the systemic failures of capitalism.
Zh: “不过，犯罪小说这个*题材*至少触及了资本主义的结构性危机。”

En: Wait, you play 'Wirrâl'?
Zh: “等等，你玩‘威勒尔’？”

En: And just like that, all trace of irony drains from his voice.
Zh: 他这么说着的时候，语调中所有的讥讽都飞快地消失了。

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. Soon you're all animatedly debating the political subtext of character creation...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。很快你又同他们激烈地辩论起来角色塑造的政治潜台词……

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. Soon you're all animatedly debating the political subtext of character creation...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。很快你又同他们激烈地辩论起来角色塑造的政治潜台词……

En: Come on, let's *really* impress them. Overturn the *race-class paradigm*. The more esoteric, the better.
Zh: 来吧，咱们可以让他们*真的*五体投地。推翻这个*种族-阶级的范例*。越掉书袋越好。

En: ... I'm just saying it's *heavily implied* that welkin are the bourgeoisie of the Wirrâl setting...
Zh: “……我的意思是，精灵在威勒尔世界观中*很明显*代表了资产阶级……”

En: But *humans* have all the most reactionary skill specialities.
Zh: “但是*人类*的特化技能都是最反动的。”

En: Right, which is why the *dweorgr* are definitely the labouring class.
Zh: “没错，这就是为什么矮人肯定象征着无产阶级。”

En: I just don't understand why welkin get all the coolest sub-races.
Zh: “我就是不明白为什么精灵的亚种族是最酷的。”

En: I reject the race-class paradigm. My personal build is a highly experimental *welkin-pygmy hybrid*.
Zh: “我拒绝这个*种族-阶级的范例*。我自己捏的角色是一个极具实验性的*精灵-侏儒混血儿*。”

En: That's a fair point. Personally, I've never understood the point of picking a human in the Wirrâl setting, but to each their own.
Zh: “说得有道理。我个人从来没弄明白过，怎么有人玩威勒尔的时候放着那么多幻想种族不选，去捏人类角色。”

En: There's no doubt about it. He nods. "I just wish they had more flexibility with their sub-races."
Zh: 年轻人点头。“这是毫无疑问的。我只希望他们能把亚种族的设定做得更灵活一些。”

En: I do hope they revise it in the next edition. But for now, I'm quite proud of the high welkin I built for our most recent campaign.
Zh: “我确实希望他们在下个版本中修改这点。但目前来说，最新范例中我构建的高阶精灵很是让我引以为豪。”

En: Combining the best race with the worst race... Not sure about the skill progressions, but I bet the role-playing opportunities are *wild*.
Zh: “把最棒的种族和最烂的种族合二为一——不知道技能树会变成什么样，但我敢打赌角色扮演的体验定会相当*狂野*。”

En: Completely counter to the spirit of 'Wirrâl'. What's even the point?
Zh: “完全违背‘威勒尔’的精神。这样做有什么意义？”

En: I still like the hill dweorgr for their Master Ironworking feat.
Zh: “高山矮人的大师级锻造特长还是很好的，我喜欢他们。”

En: Steban's welkin is so mega. He's got this umbral stave that gives him *ungodly* bonuses against shadowborn.
Zh: “史蒂文的精灵超级顶。他弄了一个日影乐谱，让他在面对暗影生物的时候有高得离谱的加成。”

En: The young man's companion says nothing, but the expression on his face reveals a begrudging respect for your experimental approach to character creation.
Zh: 年轻人的同伴一言不发。但他脸上的表情说明，对你实验性的角色创建方式，他不情愿地感到肃然起敬。

En: It's pretty awesome, I won't deny it.
Zh: “这听起来很棒，我无法否认。”

En: But seriously, the next campaign, you've got to be there. I need to see this hybrid in action.
Zh: “但说真的，下次搞活动的时候你得参加。我真想在实战中看到你的混血儿角色。”

En: In any case, I think we've heard enough. We could use someone with your perspective in the group. With just a bit more theoretical foundation, I think you'll be making real contributions.
Zh: “我想我们听得够多了。你的视角将会对我们的小组很有帮助。只要再多一些理论基础，我相信你会作出杰出的贡献。”

En: These kids are eating out of your hand, practically. Another quarter of an hour disappears. The questions come rapid-fire, but you have an answer for every one.
Zh: 这两个孩子简直字面意义上地抱着你不放。时间又流逝了十五分钟。他们像机关枪一样急切地询问你，但你答上了每个问题。

En: Now, you can sense things starting to slow down. The interview must be reaching an inflection point...
Zh: 现在，你能感觉到对话的节奏逐渐放慢。面试一定到了转折点……

En: Aha, a real old-school pedestrian materialist. Alright, we'll play along...
Zh: “啊哈，一个真正的老古板唯物主义者。好吧，我们配合你……”

En: ... but now I'm curious... Do you think there's value in reading so-called 'practical non-fiction'?
Zh: “……但现在我很好奇……你认为阅读这种所谓的‘实用非虚构书籍’真的有价值吗？”

En: So what sort of practical works are we talking about?
Zh: “那么我们说的是哪种类型的实用书籍？”

En: I enjoy thinking about truly fundamental concepts, like the alphabet for instance.
Zh: “我喜欢思考真正基础性的概念，比如说字母表。”

En: I can't say how reliable it is, but I did just read a book on pale-centric medicine.
Zh: “我最近刚读了一本和灰域医学有关的书，不过不清楚它靠不靠谱。”

En: Just consider the cockatoo for instance. How much do you really know about what they're like?
Zh: “比如说，想想凤头鹦鹉。你对它们有多少了解？”

En: Listen, books are fine and all, but to be straight with you, they're no substitute for a real story.
Zh: “听着，书籍很好，但是和你们实话实话，没有东西能代替一个真正的故事。”

En: Hmm, so you're saying you've got to have a certain practical foundation before you move onto more abstract topics. Okay, I'm following...
Zh: “哼……所以你是说，你要先建立起某种实用性的知识储备，然后才能思索更加抽象的问题。好吧，我继续听……”

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. Soon you're subjecting even the alphabet to a meticulous archaeologico-materialist critique.
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。很快你又把考古-唯物主义的缜密批判范围扩大到了字母表……

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. Soon you're subjecting even the alphabet to a meticulous archaeologico-materialist critique.
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。很快你又把考古-唯物主义的缜密批判范围扩大到了字母表……

En: That's in contrast with the International Language, which connects each letter to an abstraction.
Zh: “这和国际语言恰好相反。后者是将每个字母和一个抽象概念联系在一起。”

En: ... but even abstractions as basic as letterforms must have *some* origin in material reality...
Zh: “……但即使是字母这么基本的抽象概念，也一定能在物质世界中找到*某种*起源……”

En: A fair point. It also reminds me of one of Mazov and Nilsen's unrealized projects, a universal system of communist pictographs, one that would allow revolutionaries to communicate and organise across any language barrier.
Zh: “说得在理。这让我想起了马佐夫与尼尔森一个尚未实现的项目：世界通用的康米主义象形符号体系，它能让革命者跨越任何语言障碍，交流和组织运动。”

En: That's true. I recall from one of psycholinguistics lectures that the letter 'A' is derived from the proto-Perikarnassian glyph meant to represent a reed hut.
Zh: “没错。我记得一堂心理语言学的课上曾经讲过，字母‘A’衍生自原始佩里卡纳西斯语言中一个表现芦苇小屋的字形。”

En: Good god, have we really been talking about the alphabet for a quarter hour?  He looks down at his watch.
Zh: 他低头看看表。“老天，我们刚刚是讨论字母表讨论了十五分钟吗？”

En: It appears so.
Zh: “好像是的。”

En: Interesting, I always thought that stuff was common folk superstitions repackaged as bourgeois spiritualism, but maybe I've been overly dismissive?
Zh: “有趣。我一直觉得那东西只不过是常见的民俗迷信包装成了资产阶级的灵性主义。但或许我太小瞧它了？”

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. Soon you're debating whether a de-commodified spirituality is even possible under capitalism...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。很快你又同他们辩论起来一个去商品化的灵性是否能在资本主义下存在……

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. Soon you're debating whether a de-commodified spirituality is even possible under capitalism...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。很快你又同他们辩论起来一个去商品化的灵性是否能在资本主义下存在……

En: Probably not, is the answer. Which isn't to say it isn't *sometimes* still useful.
Zh: 答案是，或许不能。但这并不是说它*完全*无用。

En: ... but in the end, I did learn how to make some restorative tea...
Zh: “……但到了最后我确实学会了如何制作养生茶……”

En: ... but in the end I did learn how to make a poultice...
Zh: “……但到了最后我确实学会了如何制作膏药……”

En: ... but in the end I think it was mostly an excuse to pretend whiskey is good for my health...
Zh: “……但到了最后我觉得它几乎变成了一个借口，让我假装威士忌其实对我的健康有益……”

En: ... but in the end I did learn how to make a poultice...
Zh: “……但到了最后我确实学会了如何制作膏药……”

En: ... anyway it mostly seems like a way to bilk desperate people for money when they don't have access to real healthcare...
Zh: “……看起来它好像只是仗着绝望的人们无法得到真正的医疗服务，从他们身上榨取最后一点钱财……”

En: ... but then I still don't really get what this whole 'pale' thing is about, so...
Zh: “……但我还是不知道这个‘灰域’到底是什么，所以……”

En: To be perfectly honest, 'poultice' is one of those words I see around, but I have no idea what it means.
Zh: “说老实话，我经常看到‘膏药’这个词，但我完全不明白它是什么意思。”

En: Oh? Sounds nice, actually. Comrade Mazov himself preferred chamomile tea, as I'm sure you're aware.
Zh: “哦？其实听起来不错。马佐夫同志就很喜欢菊花茶，我想你肯定知道。”

En: Grain spirits are dangerous. That's why Comrade Mazov himself preferred chamomile tea, as I'm sure you're aware.
Zh: “谷物精灵很危险。这就是为什么马佐夫同志偏爱菊花茶。我想你肯定知道。”

En: A Gottwaldian would tell you that it's an inescapable fact of modernity that we can only repackage our collective history in increasingly ludicrous forms...  He nods.
Zh: 他点点头。“戈特伍德派或许会告诉你，当代社会无可回避的一个现实就是，全人类的历史在无穷无尽的包装之下，只会变得越来越荒谬可笑……”

En: There are so many theories out there, most of them ridiculous. But we'd be here all night if we tried to get into them.
Zh: “关于这点的理论不计其数，绝大多数都很荒诞。要是想详谈这些理论，我们会把整个晚上都搭进去。”

En: Thank god we're no Gottwaldians.
Zh: “感谢老天我们不是戈特伍德派。”

En: It's better not to know, that's what the wind is telling you.
Zh: 你觉得还是不要知道比较好。

En: Cockatoos? Fascinating. So tell us what you gathered from your ornithological researches.
Zh: “凤头鹦鹉？有趣。那么向我们讲讲你的鸟类学研究成果。”

En: This is a good start. They're starting to loosen up. You feel relaxed and in control. This is a subject where *you* hold all the advantages...
Zh: 这是个不错的开始。他们开始放松警惕了。你感到事态回到掌控之中。这可是*你*占尽优势的领域……

En: Now's your chance to end this interview on a high-note. This is a subject where *you* hold all the advantages...
Zh: 现在你有机会完美地结束这场面试了。这可是*你*占尽优势的领域……

En: ... and that's how I discovered that at heart I'm really just a regular Bang-Bang kind of guy.
Zh: “……那就是我如何发现，原来我只是和砰砰凤头鹦鹉一样的那种普通人。”

En: ... and that's how I discovered that, deep down, I'm really a fuckupatoo.
Zh: “……我就是那样发现了，原来在内心深处，我是一只废物鹦鹉。”

En: ... and that was the first time I saw the darkness of my own soul reflected back to me in cockatoo form.
Zh: “……那是我第一次看到自己的灵魂暗面以凤头鹦鹉的形态向我袭来。”

En: ... but really when I saw the Major Majestic in all its glory, I *knew* that was the cockatoo for me.\n
Zh: “……说真的，当我看到威严凤头鹦鹉的光辉时，我就*知道*了它会成为我命中注定的鹦鹉。”

En: ... and that's how I discovered my heraldic bird.
Zh: “……那就是我如何发掘了自己的纹章鸟。”

En: I have to say, I never realised humans and cockatoos had so much in common.
Zh: “我得说，我从来没想到过，原来人类和凤头鹦鹉还有这么多共通之处。”

En: I have to say, I never realised cockatoos could be such needy emotional disasters, just like people.
Zh: “我得说，我从来没想到过，原来凤头鹦鹉和人类一样，会在感情上如此依赖他人，”

En: I have to say, I never realised cockatoos could be so apocalyptic. I've only ever seen the grey ones.
Zh: “我得说，我从来没想到过，原来凤头鹦鹉还能是这样的末日生物。我只见过灰色的那种。”

En: I have to say, I underestimated the opulence of cockatoos. I've only ever seen the grey ones.
Zh: “我得说，我低估了凤头鹦鹉的种群多样性。我只见过灰色的那种。”

En: I have to say, I never realised humans and cockatoos had so much in common.
Zh: “我得说，我从来没想到过，原来人类和凤头鹦鹉还有这么多共通之处。”

En: It makes sense, though. Mazovianism stresses the essential continuity between humanity and nature, so anything you find in one should be reflected in the other. What's the exact quote, Uli?
Zh: “不过这很合理。马佐夫主义重视人类与自然之间的核心连续性，所以你在其中一者能够找到的内容必定在另一者中有所反映。他具体是怎么说的来着，尤利西斯？”

En: I believe it's 'Man is the mirror of the world'.
Zh: “我想应该是‘人类是世界的镜子’。”

En: Now, you can sense things starting to slow down. The interview must be reaching an inflection point...
Zh: 现在，你能感觉到对话的节奏逐渐放慢。面试一定到了转折点……

En: In any case, I think we've heard enough. We could use someone with your perspective in the group. With just a bit more theoretical foundation, I think you'll be making real contributions.
Zh: “我想我们听得够多了。你的视角将会对我们的小组很有帮助。只要再多一些理论基础，我相信你会作出杰出的贡献。”

En: These kids are eating out of your hand, practically. Another quarter of an hour disappears. The questions come rapid-fire, but you have an answer for every one.
Zh: 这两个孩子简直字面意义上地抱着你不放。时间又流逝了十五分钟。他们像机关枪一样急切地询问你，但你答上了每个问题。

En: Okay, we'll bite. The young man and his companion look at one another, then turn back to you. "What sort of stories are we talking about?"
Zh: 年轻人和同伴看了看彼此，然后转向你。“好吧，我们上钩了。我们要讲什么故事？”

En: Let's just say the human body starts to do some weird things when it's been in a tree for seven days...
Zh: “我们先来说说，人类的身体在大树里呆了七天之后，会发生什么怪事……”

En: I'll tell you how I infiltrated the Wild Pines for my investigation...
Zh: “我来讲我是怎么潜入野松公司进行调查的……”

En: Let me tell you about a little *incident* I had with this woman at my hostel...
Zh: “让我来告诉你们，我和旅社里住着的另一个女人发生的一起小小*事故*……”

En: I'll tell you about the time I woke up and completely forgot what money is...
Zh: “我和你们说说，我是怎么早上醒来，完全忘了钱是什么东西的……”

En: You ever heard of a thing called 'sudden onset acute amnesia'?
Zh: “你们听说过‘突发急性失忆症’吗？”

En: Sounds like old-fashioned revolutionary espionage. Let's see where this goes...
Zh: “听起来像是老式革命谍战戏。我们来听听看故事如何发展……”

En: A story with an erotico-political angle. Let's see where this goes...
Zh: “一个同时带有性和政治元素故事。我们来听听看故事如何发展……”

En: Oh, we're getting into the psychiatric pathologies of life under capital. Let's see where this goes...
Zh: “哦，说到资本主义制度下生命的精神病理学了。我们来听听看故事如何发展……”

En: You've got our attention. Let's see where this goes...
Zh: “你抓住我们的注意力了。我们来听听看故事如何发展……”

En: Yes, I'm rather curious myself.
Zh: “是的，我自己也很好奇。”

En: Now you're getting somewhere. These boys aren't even bothering to contain their interest. They want to see how you weave the threads of this story together...
Zh: 你做得不错。这两个男孩已经没有心思假装他们不感兴趣了，只想看你如何灵巧地将散碎的情节编织成片……

En: You've got them. They're starting to loosen up. You're relaxed and in control. You deftly weave every piece of the story together...
Zh: 你吸引住他们了。他们开始放松警惕。你感到事态回到掌控之中。你灵巧地将散碎的情节编织成片……

En: ... Okay, but when you say he *spoke* to you, you mean metaphorically, right?
Zh: “……好吧，但当你说他对你*开口*的时候，你是在打比方吗？”

En: ... I'm just trying to imagine what it would have been like had he died in the summer...
Zh: “……我在试图想象，要是他死在了夏天，事情会变成什么样子……”

En: ... but I still don't see what the lady driver had to do with the strike stand-off...
Zh: “……但我还是没明白，女司机和罢工对峙到底有什么关系？”

En: ... so is it typical for men of your background to use that, um, formulation?
Zh: “……所以说像你们这样背景的人，会常常使用那个，呃，公式吗？”

En: I'm just trying to imagine what it would be like to wake up with no concept of what money is...
Zh: “我在试图想象，醒过来的时候完全忘了钱是什么东西，那得是什么感受……”

En: Right, of course. I'm really talking about my top-notch forensics skills.
Zh: “没错，当然是打比方。我只是在说我一流的法医学技巧。”

En: No, I mean literally.
Zh: “不，我是说他真的开口了。”

En: The work he did on the autopsy was quite good, I can confirm.
Zh: “他的尸检工作做得很好，我可以作证。”

En: So have you figured out his real identity?
Zh: “那你找出他的真实身份了吗？”

En: Another quarter of an hour disappears. The conversation bounces back and forth. Whatever their pretensions, it's clear these two have been craving something *real*.
Zh: 又过了半个小时。你们来来回回地对话。不管这两人想装成什么样子，很明显他们急切地想听到一些真正有意思的东西。

En: Now, you can sense things starting to slow down. Time to wrap this tale up...
Zh: 现在，你能感觉到对话的节奏逐渐放慢。该收尾了……

En: Ah, so there *is* a supra-natural angle to this case...
Zh: “啊，所以说这起案件*居然*涉及了超自然力量……”

En: Not pretty. Eventually the force of gravity would have caused the rest of the body to separate from the head. There would have been *very little* for us to work with...
Zh: “其实并非如此。最终重力将会导致死者的身体与头颅分离，这样我们就只能找到*一点点*线索了……”

En: Kim's right. There's no way we would've been able to determine the true cause of death...
Zh: “金说得对。要是这样，我们就无论如何都没法发现真正的死因了……”

En: It would have been a mess, let me tell you. No way I would've been able to find the true cause of death...
Zh: “我可以告诉你，那会变得一团糟。我就无论如何都没法发现真正的死因了……”

En: Wait, are you saying the hanged man wasn't really *hanged*?
Zh: “等等，你是说，吊人并不是被*吊死*的？”

En: In all fairness, the whole situation is rather complicated.
Zh: “老实说，这整件事情非常复杂。”

En: That's just it, though. She was just one more pawn, just like how the Wild Pines rep tried to play me.
Zh: “其实就是这么回事。她不过是一个棋子，野松公司的代表利用她，就像她也试图利用我一样。”

En: Yeah, I'm still trying to piece the whole thing together, but I can't get it to add up yet.
Zh: “是的，我还在试图推导整件事，但目前还没有明确的结论。”

En: I'm not even sure there *is* a connection, but I needed to get the Pines' cooperation *somehow*.
Zh: “我甚至不知道这些事情之前*有没有*联系，但我得*想办法*让野松公司配合。”

En: Clearly there's a lot of ins-and-outs. It almost reminds me of a Dick Mullen novel.
Zh: “这件事有许多波折起伏呀，甚至让我想到了迪克马伦小说。”

En: Ah, right. You were playing the long game.
Zh: “啊，没错，你在放长线钓大鱼。”

En: A great question.
Zh: “一个好问题。”

En: No, my brain came up with that all by itself.
Zh: “不，我的大脑单枪匹马地把它想出来的。”

En: You know, in a weird way, I think she was into it.
Zh: “你知道吗，我觉得她诡异地享受着这件事情。”

En: Honestly, I think the main problem was my shirt. If it had been just a *little* cleaner, I probably could've pulled it off.
Zh: “老实说，我觉得主要问题在于我的衣着。假如我更卫生那么*一点点*，恐怕我就成功了。”

En: So you're saying it didn't suck to interrogate her later? I find that unlikely...
Zh: “所以你的意思，你没有因为没能审问她而感到沮丧？听起来不太合理嘛……”

En: Strange. I thought that women were attracted to a certain amount of revolutionary musk...
Zh: “奇怪。我还以为那个女人被革命的麝香深深吸引……”

En: Maybe it's a question of degree.
Zh: “可能和程度有关。”

En: If you encountered it in a novel, you'd say it sounded like a plot contrivance, or like a fantasy.
Zh: “如果你在小说里读到这种情节，你一定会觉得这个转折逻辑堪称强词夺理，或者根本就是臆想。”

En: Listen, I wouldn't believe it either, but I swear it's the truth.
Zh: “听着，我也不敢相信，但这是真的。”

En: But isn't the point that money is itself just a collective fantasy?
Zh: “但问题不就在于，金钱本身就是一个集体的臆想？”

En: Either that, or an *extremely* convincing act...
Zh: “要么是那样，要么就是一起*极有*欺骗性的表演……”

En: Perhaps, but it's a fantasy that *pays* for things, like your hostel bill...
Zh: “或许是的，但这个臆想能够帮你*支付*一些东西，比如你的旅社账单……”

En: No, it's just like Mazov wrote: 'The freeman of the future will be he who awakens in perfect ignorance of Capital'.
Zh: “不，这就和马佐夫说的一样：‘只有在资本主义的完美无知中清醒过来的那些，才能成为未来的自由人。’”

En: Wait, Steban, are you saying the gendarme is some kind of *future communist*?
Zh: “等等，史蒂文，你是想说这位警官是某种未来的康米主义者吗？”

En: ... but how could communism and love *both* have killed him?
Zh: “……但康米主义和爱怎么能*一起*杀了他呢？”

En: ... but I'm still not sure how *communism* could have killed him...
Zh: “……但我还是不知道*康米主义*是怎么杀了他的……”

En: ... but I'm still not sure how *love* did him in...
Zh: “……但我还是不知道*爱情*是怎么害死他的……”

En: ... and we still don't know what happened to the rest of his armour...
Zh: “……但我们还是不知道他剩下的盔甲去了哪里……”

En: ... so anyway, my current theory is that she's somewhere along the coast.
Zh: “……反正不管怎样，我目前的推测就是，她逃到了海岸边的某个地方。”

En: ... so anyway, I think there's still a shot. But in the meantime I'm just focussing on my casework.
Zh: “……反正不管怎样，我还有机会尝试。但与此同时我正专注于侦察工作。”

En: ... and anyway, even if you could somehow *unpeel* time, it wouldn't be the *you* you who gets rich from those investments...
Zh: “……不管怎样，即使你能通过某种方式剥除时间的外壳，能从这些投资中获取财富的人也不会是*你*了……”

En: Makes sense. It doesn't seem like there's anywhere else she could have gone.
Zh: “合情合理。看起来她也没有别的地方可去了。”

En: Makes sense. Based on my experience, you've got to let her see that you've grown, ideologically speaking...
Zh: “合情合理。基于我的经验，你得让她看到你成长了，意识形态层面上的……”

En: But that would imply that whenever you unpeel time you leave behind some kind of *doppelgänger* in now-time...
Zh: “但这又意味着，不管何时，当你*剥除*时间的外壳，你都会把某种*二重身*留在当下……”

En: I think he's saying it was communism that *actually* committed the murder, but it was love that brought him to that point...
Zh: “我想他的意思是，*真正*杀了他的是康米主义，但爱情驱使他陷入圈套……”

En: There has to be some other element to it. We don't have the full story yet...
Zh: “这一定还有些别的因素。我们暂时还不清楚事情的全貌……”

En: There has to be some other element to it. We don't have the full story yet...
Zh: “这一定还有些别的因素。我们暂时还不清楚事情的全貌……”

En: It will turn up, I'm sure. In a way, it wants to be found...
Zh: “它会出现的，我很确信。不管以什么方式，我们都会找到它的……”

En: Right, that's basically it. But like I said, it's only a working theory...
Zh: “是的，基本就是这样。但我刚才也说了，这只是一种推测……”

En: Perhaps. But that thread of the story is still unresolved...
Zh: “有可能。但这个思路还有些问题。”

En: Good god, have we really been talking about 'unpeeling time' for a quarter hour?
Zh: “老天，我们刚刚是讨论剥除时间讨论了十五分钟吗？”

En: His "experience" with these matters is painfully limited, not that he realises it.
Zh: 他对于这种事情的“经验”少得可怜，但他自己并未意识到这一点。

En: Steban's right. If it's going to happen, it will happen.
Zh: “史蒂文是对的。该发生的事情都会发生。”

En: I suppose we'll have to leave it there. But listen, gendarme: We could use someone with your *breadth of expertise*. With just a little more theoretical background, I think you'll be able to make some real contributions.
Zh: “我想我们得停在这里了。但是听着，警官：你的博学多闻对于我们的阅读小组将会有所裨益。只要再多一些理论基础，我相信你会作出杰出的贡献。”

En: Yes, I would say he's got serious potential at least.
Zh: “没错，我得说他至少有着极高的潜力。”

En: Besides, it's inconceivable that we'd turn down a fellow Wirrâl enthusiast...
Zh: “另外，我们怎么也不能拒绝一位热爱威勒尔的同好……”

En: Okay, gendarme. The young man rubs his cheek gingerly. "You've earned your place. Welcome to the most ideologically advanced materialist reading group in Martinaise!"\n
Zh: “好了，警官。”年轻人小心翼翼地摸了摸脸颊。“你赢得了一席之地。欢迎来到全马丁内斯意识形态最超前的唯物主义阅读小组！”

En: Alright, you maniac. The young man takes a moment to massage his bruised jaw. "Let's see if you can use your brain as well as you use your fists."
Zh: “可以了，你这狂人。”年轻人摸摸他淤青的下巴。“让我们来看看你动脑的能力和你动手的能力相比如何。”

En: And with that, welcome to the most ideologically advanced materialist reading group in Martinaise!
Zh: “那么，欢迎来到全马丁内斯意识形态最超前的唯物主义阅读小组！”

En: Here's your first assignment. It's an overview of infra-materialist theory. A little basic, as you'll see, but one has to start somewhere.
Zh: “现在，收下你的第一份作业。这是一篇关于亚唯物主义理论的综述。如你所见，这很基础，但是学习总要有个开始。”

En: Guess it's time to see what this 'book' stuff is all about.
Zh: “看来是时候弄明白这个‘书籍’到底是什么东西了。”

En: I'll add it to my very extensive reading list.
Zh: “我将会把它加入我广博的书单。”

En: (Say nothing, just take the book.)
Zh: （什么也不说。默默接过书本。）

En: It's sweet how excited you are. Come back when you're done. We'll be here pretty much every night after 22.00.
Zh: “看到你这么兴奋真让我开心。等你看完了就回来。我们基本上每天晚上22时之后都会在这里集会。”

En: You're going to fit right in, I think. Come back when you're done. We'll be here pretty much every night after 22.00.
Zh: “你会融入我们的小组的。等你看完了就回来。我们基本上每天晚上22时之后都会在这里集会。”

En: Come back when you're done. He gives you a brief nod as he hands you the slim guide. "We'll be here pretty much every night after 22.00."
Zh: 将那本薄薄的书籍递给你之后，他向你点点头。“等你看完了就回来。我们基本上每天晚上22时之后都会在这里集会。”

En: Do be sure to take your time with the reading. We'll be eager to hear your thoughts.
Zh: “不过请尽量快些读。我们很想知道你的见解。”

En: Nope, still no words. Your confidence account is overdrawn. What are you even doing here?
Zh: 不，还是一言不发。你的自信已被透支。你到底在这里做什么？

En: Now you're just psyching yourself out. You've read plenty of books. Maybe try again once you've got your wits about you?
Zh: 你只是在自己吓自己。你已经读了很多书了。或许等你准备好了再试试？

En: Maybe now's a good time to call it an evening. Why don't you find a good book, clear your mind, and come back when you're refreshed?
Zh: 就这样结束今晚的活动吧。何不找一本好书，清空思绪，等你看完了再回来？

En: Have you considered reading a *book*? Those tend to have *lots* of words in them...
Zh: 你考虑过阅读一本*书籍*吗？一般都有很多字数。

En: Is it getting warm in here? There seems to be a little pool of sweat forming in the depression of your lower back.
Zh: 这里是不是变得暖和起来了？你的后背上似乎沁出了汗珠。

En: Okay, you got this. You *got* this. Just... open your mouth, and say the words...
Zh: 好，你能做到的。你*能*。只要……张开嘴，说出……

En: Ehhk...
Uhhm...
Hrrrgh...
What are those sounds? They're like the last mumblings of a mortally wounded badger.
Zh: 这是什么声音？听起来就像一头垂死的獾的哀鸣。

En: There's nothing you can do. It's like your lips have been glued together.
Zh: 你无能为力。仿佛你的嘴唇被黏在了一起。

En: Everything in order, detective? The lieutenant gives you a concerned look.
Zh: 警督忧虑地看你一眼。“你还好吧，警探？”

En: Nod your head yes.
Zh: 点头表示你还好。

En: The young man gives you a concerned look.
Zh: 年轻人忧虑地看你一眼。

En: Shake your head no.
Zh: 摇头表示你不好。

En: That's a 'yes,' I take it? Can you give me an affirmative?
Zh: “点头是‘好’的意思吗？你能否确认一下？”

En: What are you worried about? You've read plenty of books. Why don't you take a little pause, come back once your mind's clear?
Zh: 你在担心什么？你读了不少书了。何不暂停一会儿，等头脑清醒了再回来？
